Option C is wrong because regional persistent disks replicate within one region only and are not a backup mechanism. Option D is correct because Backup and DR Service supports consistency groups for multi-disk VMs and Backup Vault provides governed, cross-region retention with application-aware recovery.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because file-system freeze alone does not produce application-consistent backups across multiple disks; ordering is needed.
Option B is wrong because hourly block image copies are slow, lack application consistency, and require custom restore tooling.
Option C is wrong because regional persistent disks replicate within one region only and are not a backup mechanism.
Option D is correct because Backup and DR Service supports consistency groups for multi-disk VMs and Backup Vault provides governed, cross-region retention with application-aware recovery.

questions.question: A team must select storage for a workload that ingests 5 TB of small binary objects per day, requires consistent single-digit-millisecond read latency from web frontends, and needs strong consistency on writes. Which storage system is most appropriate?
options: Cloud Storage with the Standard storage class, fronted by a Cloud CDN distribution and presigned URLs for direct reads to minimize the latency between web frontends and object retrieval
BigQuery with object table references over a Cloud Storage bucket so the binary content is accessible by SQL while the actual bytes are served from the bucket on demand
Filestore Enterprise tier with NFS clients on the web frontends, providing block-level small-object reads with sub-millisecond latency and full POSIX compliance for binary payloads
Cloud Spanner with the BYTES column type and regional configuration to keep data in one region while benefiting from Spanner's globally consistent reads for binary blobs

Option B is wrong because object tables expose Cloud Storage metadata for SQL and do not improve binary read latency. Option C is correct because Filestore Enterprise delivers single-digit-millisecond NFS read latency, strong consistency, and POSIX semantics suitable for small-object reads. Option D is wrong because Spanner is not optimized for binary blob storage and BYTES columns are expensive at multi-TB scale.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Cloud Storage has higher per-request latency than block or file storage and is optimized for throughput, not single-digit-millisecond consistent reads.
Option B is wrong because object tables expose Cloud Storage metadata for SQL and do not improve binary read latency.
Option C is correct because Filestore Enterprise delivers single-digit-millisecond NFS read latency, strong consistency, and POSIX semantics suitable for small-object reads.
Option D is wrong because Spanner is not optimized for binary blob storage and BYTES columns are expensive at multi-TB scale.

questions.question: An ML team needs a Cloud Storage bucket whose data growth is unpredictable but cost-sensitive; access patterns flip between hot and cold without warning. They want Google to manage tier transitions automatically without lifecycle rules. Which feature should they enable?
options: Object lifecycle management with a complex set of conditional rules that transition objects between Standard, Nearline, Coldline, and Archive based on age, size, and read counters per object
Bucket-level Object Versioning with automatic noncurrent-time transitions to Coldline so older versions move down the tiers and current versions stay where they were written initially
Autoclass on the bucket so Cloud Storage automatically moves objects between Standard, Nearline, Coldline, and Archive based on access patterns, with no early-deletion charges
Use a dual-region bucket so reads are served from the closest region and write costs are absorbed by the multi-region replication infrastructure independent of lifecycle policies
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because lifecycle rules require predictable patterns and rules; this is what Autoclass eliminates. Option B is wrong because Object Versioning addresses retention of past versions, not tiering of current data based on access frequency. Option C is correct because Autoclass automates transitions across all four classes based on observed access, with no early-deletion fees for class transitions made by Autoclass. Option D is wrong because dual-region buckets address regional availability, not cost management for unpredictable access.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because lifecycle rules require predictable patterns and rules; this is what Autoclass eliminates.
Option B is wrong because Object Versioning addresses retention of past versions, not tiering of current data based on access frequency.
Option C is correct because Autoclass automates transitions across all four classes based on observed access, with no early-deletion fees for class transitions made by Autoclass.
Option D is wrong because dual-region buckets address regional availability, not cost management for unpredictable access.

Use an N2D machine type because AMD-based instances always provide higher AVX-512 throughput than equivalent Intel-based families for AVX-512 floating-point trading workloads
Use an E2 machine type sized to 96 vCPUs because E2 offers the strongest single-thread performance among general-purpose families and the lowest sustained-use cost on Google Cloud
Use a T2D machine type because Tau VMs offer the highest AVX-512 instruction throughput for memory-bandwidth-bound trading analytics in Google's general-purpose lineup

Option C is wrong because E2 trades performance for cost and is not aimed at latency-sensitive trading.

questions.question: A batch image-processing pipeline needs the lowest cost on Google Cloud and tolerates being interrupted at any time with a 30-second grace period. Which compute configuration should be used?

A Cloud Run service with concurrency set to 1 so each image gets a dedicated container instance and minimum instances set to 0 to avoid charges while idle
A GKE Standard cluster with default node pools using on-demand N2 instances and the Cluster Autoscaler set to scale to zero between batches once the pipeline completes
A Managed Instance Group of Spot VMs with the pipeline structured to checkpoint frequently, plus a shutdown script that gracefully drains work within the 30-second preemption notice

Option B is wrong because Cloud Run with concurrency 1 is suited to request/response, not large-scale batch image processing, and it is not the cheapest option per CPU-hour. Option C is wrong because on-demand N2 nodes still cost much more than Spot VMs. Option D is correct because Spot VMs offer up to roughly 60-91% discount and the workload's interruption tolerance and 30-second shutdown handling fit Spot's preemption model.

Option B is wrong because Cloud Run with concurrency 1 is suited to request/response, not large-scale batch image processing, and it is not the cheapest option per CPU-hour.
Option C is wrong because on-demand N2 nodes still cost much more than Spot VMs.

questions.question: A platform team is hardening a Cloud Run service that must read from Filestore over NFS and call Cloud SQL privately. Which TWO Cloud Run configurations are required to support both backends?

Use Cloud Run for Anthos on a GKE cluster so the underlying nodes can mount the Filestore share via DaemonSet and the Cloud Run services can read the mount through hostPath bindings
Use the second-generation Cloud Run execution environment so that kernel features required for NFS volume mounts and IPv4 transport for Cloud SQL Private IP are available
Configure Direct VPC egress or a Serverless VPC Access connector so that the service can reach the Filestore and Cloud SQL Private IPs inside the customer VPC

Option B is wrong because Cloud Run for Anthos was renamed and hostPath bindings are not exposed as described. Option C is correct because the second-generation execution environment provides the kernel features needed for NFS volume mounts and supports VPC connectivity. Option D is correct because Direct VPC egress or a Serverless VPC Access connector is required for Cloud Run to reach Filestore and Cloud SQL Private IPs.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Cloud Run jobs are for batch and Cloud Storage Fuse mounts buckets, not Filestore.
Option B is wrong because Cloud Run for Anthos was renamed and hostPath bindings are not exposed as described.
Option C is correct because the second-generation execution environment provides the kernel features needed for NFS volume mounts and supports VPC connectivity.

questions.question: An enterprise running 600 Compute Engine VMs needs to apply OS-level security patches on a defined maintenance window across Windows and Linux fleets, with rollback if a patch causes service errors. Which Google Cloud service should be used?

Use Cloud Scheduler to invoke a Cloud Function that runs gcloud compute ssh and apt-get upgrade or Windows Update commands across all instances, then redeploys via Deployment Manager if anything fails
Use VM Manager patch management to define patch jobs across selected zones, pre-patch and post-patch scripts for rollback, and patch deployments aligned to maintenance windows for the fleet
Use Config Connector custom resources that declare desired patch baselines and have the operator reconcile each VM weekly through SSH automation managed by the Anthos Config Management agent

Option B is wrong because Cloud Function-based SSH at hundreds of VMs is fragile, not designed for OS patching, and lacks structured pre/post checks. Option C is correct because VM Manager patch management is purpose-built for OS patching across Windows and Linux, supports pre/post-patch scripts, and integrates with maintenance windows. Option D is wrong because Config Connector targets Google Cloud resources, not OS-level patching on VMs.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because monthly image rebakes do not provide on-demand patch jobs or rollback hooks and are operationally heavy at fleet scale.
Option B is wrong because Cloud Function-based SSH at hundreds of VMs is fragile, not designed for OS patching, and lacks structured pre/post checks.

Option D is wrong because Config Connector targets Google Cloud resources, not OS-level patching on VMs.

questions.question: A FinTech application requires a GKE cluster where pods are isolated to dedicated hardware not shared with other Google Cloud customers for compliance with a regulator's tenancy clause. Which configuration meets the requirement with the least operational burden?
options: Use GKE Standard with node pools running on sole-tenant nodes configured with a node affinity label, and bind workload Pod scheduling to those nodes via taints, tolerations, and nodeSelectors
Use GKE Autopilot in the regulated region because Autopilot automatically reserves dedicated hardware for any pod that requests guaranteed QoS class through resource requests and limits
Use a private GKE cluster with shielded nodes and Workload Identity, since the regulator's tenancy clause is satisfied as long as the nodes are encrypted with customer-managed keys
Use GKE Standard with confidential VMs as nodes, ensuring memory encryption is active so the regulator considers the workload tenancy-isolated for compliance purposes

Option B is wrong because Autopilot does not run on sole-tenant nodes and QoS class does not imply hardware isolation. Option C is wrong because CMEK addresses key custody, not hardware tenancy. Option D is wrong because Confidential VMs encrypt memory but still run on shared physical hardware.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because sole-tenant nodes provide dedicated physical hardware, and node pools with affinity labels along with taints and tolerations bind pods to those nodes in GKE Standard.
Option B is wrong because Autopilot does not run on sole-tenant nodes and QoS class does not imply hardware isolation.
Option C is wrong because CMEK addresses key custody, not hardware tenancy.
Option D is wrong because Confidential VMs encrypt memory but still run on shared physical hardware.

questions.question: A team wants the simplest GKE option for running stateless web services without managing nodes, node pools, or cluster autoscaling. They are willing to follow Google's opinionated defaults in exchange for less operational overhead. Which option is best?
options: GKE Autopilot, where Google manages nodes, scaling, security defaults, and bin-packing, charging per pod resource requests rather than per node, with a SLA on pod uptime
GKE Standard with the node auto-provisioning feature enabled and the Cluster Autoscaler tuned to scale to zero so node management cost is minimized between bursts
Anthos clusters on bare metal, since on-prem clusters provide stronger defaults than managed GKE and reduce the team's need to operate nodes through declarative configuration
GKE Edge clusters running on Google Distributed Cloud, where Google maintains the hardware and the team only manages the control plane configuration through Cloud Console

Option B is wrong because GKE Standard still requires the team to manage node pools, machine types, and patch windows even with autoscaling. Option C is wrong because Anthos on bare metal increases operational responsibility rather than reducing it. Option D is wrong because Distributed Cloud Edge is for on-premises and edge deployments, not the simplest cloud GKE option.

Option B is wrong because GKE Standard still requires the team to manage node pools, machine types, and patch windows even with autoscaling.
Option C is wrong because Anthos on bare metal increases operational responsibility rather than reducing it.
Option D is wrong because Distributed Cloud Edge is for on-premises and edge deployments, not the simplest cloud GKE option.

questions.question: A microservices platform on GKE must give each Kubernetes ServiceAccount least-privilege access to Google Cloud APIs without distributing service-account keys to pods. Which feature should be configured?
options: Workload Identity Federation for GKE, which lets Kubernetes ServiceAccounts impersonate Google service accounts using short-lived tokens issued by the cluster's identity provider
Bind a single Google service account JSON key as a Kubernetes secret mounted into all pods, restricting access through Kubernetes RBAC over the secret to minimize blast radius
Use node service accounts attached to each node pool and rely on pod-level NetworkPolicies to constrain which pods can call which Google APIs based on pod-source IPs
Run a sidecar in each pod that performs Application Default Credentials login against the metadata server every minute and caches Google OAuth tokens for the workload
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Workload Identity Federation for GKE binds Kubernetes ServiceAccounts to Google service accounts with short-lived tokens and no static keys. Option B is wrong because distributing a long-lived JSON key to all pods is the opposite of least privilege and has no per-ServiceAccount scoping. Option C is wrong because node service accounts grant the same identity to every pod on the node and NetworkPolicies do not authenticate to Google APIs. Option D is wrong because side cars cannot meaningfully scope per-Kubernetes-ServiceAccount access without Workload Identity.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because Workload Identity Federation for GKE binds Kubernetes ServiceAccounts to Google service accounts with short-lived tokens and no static keys.
Option B is wrong because distributing a long-lived JSON key to all pods is the opposite of least privilege and has no per-ServiceAccount scoping.
Option C is wrong because node service accounts grant the same identity to every pod on the node and NetworkPolicies do not authenticate to Google APIs.
Option D is wrong because side cars cannot meaningfully scope per-Kubernetes-ServiceAccount access without Workload Identity.

questions.question: A workload running on Cloud Run handles webhook events spiking from 0 to 10,000 requests per second within seconds and must respond with sub-200ms p99 latency. The team observes excessive cold starts. Which configuration changes will most effectively reduce cold start impact?
options: Switch the service to first-generation execution environment, disable CPU boost, and set concurrency to 1 to ensure each container only handles one event at a time during cold starts
Migrate the workload to Cloud Run jobs and trigger jobs from a Pub/Sub push subscription per webhook event so each job runs in an isolated container that does not experience cold starts
Move the entire workload to Compute Engine MIGs because Cloud Run does not provide sufficient performance for webhook receivers handling spikes of more than a few thousand requests per second
Set minimum instances to a warm pool, enable startup CPU boost, raise concurrency above 1 to amortize containers, and ensure HTTP/2 end-to-end so connections are reused efficiently
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because first-generation environment is being deprecated and concurrency of 1 maximizes container count rather than minimizing cold start impact. Option B is wrong because Cloud Run jobs are not request-driven and per-event jobs incur the same or worse startup cost. Option C is wrong because Cloud Run scales to thousands of containers within seconds, and rewriting to MIGs adds significant operational burden. Option D is correct because minimum instances provide a warm pool, CPU boost shortens cold starts, concurrency above 1 reuses warm containers, and HTTP/2 reuses connections.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because first-generation environment is being deprecated and concurrency of 1 maximizes container count rather than minimizing cold start impact.
Option B is wrong because Cloud Run jobs are not request-driven and per-event jobs incur the same or worse startup cost.
Option C is wrong because Cloud Run scales to thousands of containers within seconds, and rewriting to MIGs adds significant operational burden.
Option D is correct because minimum instances provide a warm pool, CPU boost shortens cold starts, concurrency above 1 reuses warm containers, and HTTP/2 reuses connections.

questions.question: A legacy enterprise application stack uses VMware ESXi with proprietary vSphere features such as DRS, vCenter, and VMware NSX. Use Migrate to Containers to convert each VMware VM into a Kubernetes deployment on GKE Autopilot, retaining only the application binaries while discarding the VMware control plane
Use Compute Engine with sole-tenant nodes running a custom nested VMware ESXi image, allowing the team to manage vCenter and NSX themselves on top of Compute Engine VMs
Use Cloud VMware DR Service, which mirrors on-premises ESXi clusters into Google Cloud and exposes them as Compute Engine instances after failover with a vSphere-compatible API

Option B is wrong because Migrate to Containers requires application changes and discards VMware-specific features. Option C is wrong because nested ESXi on Compute Engine is unsupported for production and lacks vendor support. Option D is wrong because there is no Google product named Cloud VMware DR Service; VMware DR is built on top of VMware Engine.

Option B is wrong because Migrate to Containers requires application changes and discards VMware-specific features.
Option C is wrong because nested ESXi on Compute Engine is unsupported for production and lacks vendor support.
Option D is wrong because there is no Google product named Cloud VMware DR Service; VMware DR is built on top of VMware Engine.

questions.question: An e-commerce platform expects predictable steady-state Compute Engine usage of 200 vCPUs N2 across two regions for the next three years, with bursty traffic peaks above that baseline. Which commitment model minimizes cost while preserving burst capacity?
options: Buy 3-year resource-based committed use discounts covering 300 vCPUs total to ensure even surge traffic is covered by the discount, then let unused commitment apply during off-peak hours
Buy 1-year flexible committed use discounts (flex CUDs) for 300 vCPUs across all N2 SKUs in both regions, since flex CUDs cover sustained and bursty usage with the deepest discounts available
Buy 3-year resource-based committed use discounts for the 200 vCPU steady-state baseline and pay on-demand for burst above the baseline, taking advantage of sustained-use discounts on bursts
Buy 1-year spend-based committed use discounts equal to twice the steady-state spend so most of the burst traffic is also covered by the commitment with no on-demand cost overruns
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because over-committing to 300 vCPUs wastes spend when bursts are infrequent. Option B is wrong because flex CUDs offer smaller discounts than resource-based 3-year CUDs and are best for variable workloads, not steady-state. Option C is correct because 3-year resource-based CUDs on the baseline maximize discount on guaranteed usage, while on-demand with sustained-use discounts covers the burst above the baseline economically. Option D is wrong because over-committing 1-year spend at twice the baseline still wastes capital and offers shallower discounts than 3-year resource-based CUDs.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because over-committing to 300 vCPUs wastes spend when bursts are infrequent.
Option B is wrong because flex CUDs offer smaller discounts than resource-based 3-year CUDs and are best for variable workloads, not steady-state.

Option C is correct because 3-year resource-based CUDs on the baseline maximize discount on guaranteed usage, while on-demand with sustained-use discounts covers the burst above the baseline economically.
Option D is wrong because over-committing 1-year spend at twice the baseline still wastes capital and offers shallower discounts than 3-year resource-based CUDs.

Cloud Composer DAGs that orchestrate Vertex AI custom training jobs by invoking the gcloud ai custom-jobs API directly from BashOperators on a recurring schedule

Use Cloud TPU v5p pods through Vertex AI training, leveraging AI Hypercomputer's optimized ICI interconnect, MaxText, and accelerator-aware schedulers for dense LLM training
Use Cloud Run jobs with GPU enabled and burst across regions to take advantage of fractional GPU pricing for large training runs across L4 accelerators in multiple zones

Use Vertex AI Feature Store to serve and persist managed feature data and use Vertex ML Metadata to record artifact lineage and runtime parameters across pipeline executions

Run the fine-tuning as a custom training job on Compute Engine A3 instances with NVIDIA H100 GPUs and write a custom Flask serving wrapper exposed through an external load balancer
Build a Vertex AI custom training job using PyTorch and TensorRT-LLM to fine-tune the base Gemini weights, then serve the model from Cloud Run with GPU and HTTP endpoints

questions.question: A team is operating a Vertex AI training run across 1,024 TPU v5p chips using AI Hypercomputer. They want to maximize accelerator utilization and reduce idle time during preemption or hardware faults. Which feature should they use?
options: Configure each TPU pod slice as a separate Vertex AI custom job with manual scheduling and use Cloud Scheduler to restart any pod slice that fails during the training run
Train with reduced batch sizes and chip count so failures are statistically less likely, accepting longer wall-clock training time as the cost of higher per-step reliability
Use Vertex AI custom training with the AI Hypercomputer-managed resource scheduler and Dynamic Workload Scheduler to provision and reschedule large blocks of accelerators atomically
Run the training on Compute Engine GPU VMs instead of TPUs because Vertex AI managed training does not support large TPU v5p pod slices for jobs with more than 256 chips
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because manual scheduling across 1,024 chips defeats AI Hypercomputer's coordinated scheduling and is error prone. Option B is wrong because reducing chip count and batch size penalizes throughput and is not how Google recommends maximizing utilization. Option C is correct because Dynamic Workload Scheduler in AI Hypercomputer schedules large accelerator blocks atomically and handles hardware faults to reduce idle time. Option D is wrong because Vertex AI managed training supports TPU v5p pods well beyond 256 chips through AI Hypercomputer.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because manual scheduling across 1,024 chips defeats AI Hypercomputer's coordinated scheduling and is error prone.
Option B is wrong because reducing chip count and batch size penalizes throughput and is not how Google recommends maximizing utilization.
Option C is correct because Dynamic Workload Scheduler in AI Hypercomputer schedules large accelerator blocks atomically and handles hardware faults to reduce idle time.
Option D is wrong because Vertex AI managed training supports TPU v5p pods well beyond 256 chips through AI Hypercomputer.

questions.question: A team must optimize inference cost for a Gemini-powered text-classification API that handles 50 QPS during business hours and near-zero traffic at night. Which TWO Vertex AI tactics best reduce idle cost while preserving steady-state performance?
options: Deploy the model to a Vertex AI endpoint with dedicated provisioned throughput sized for peak QPS so that the team can lock in capacity even during idle hours for predictable cost
Use Vertex AI Model Garden pay-as-you-go API endpoints for Gemini, which bill per input and output token only when requests are sent and incur no idle-time cost overnight
Run the workload on a fixed-size GKE cluster with three GPU nodes that handle the peak load and remain provisioned at night to avoid GPU acquisition delays in the morning
Enable a minimum-replica count of zero on the Vertex AI online endpoint and configure autoscaling to scale up during business hours so capacity follows actual request traffic
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because provisioned throughput sized for peak guarantees idle cost overnight, which is not optimal for spiky traffic. Option B is correct because pay-as-you-go Gemini API endpoints bill per token only on actual requests, ideal for spiky traffic with near-zero idle. Option C is wrong because a fixed three-GPU cluster wastes resources at night and adds management overhead. Option D is correct because configuring autoscaling with a minimum-replica count of zero allows the endpoint to scale down during idle hours and back up under business-hour load.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because provisioned throughput sized for peak guarantees idle cost overnight, which is not optimal for spiky traffic.
Option B is correct because pay-as-you-go Gemini API endpoints bill per token only on actual requests, ideal for spiky traffic with near-zero idle.
Option C is wrong because a fixed three-GPU cluster wastes resources at night and adds management overhead.
Option D is correct because configuring autoscaling with a minimum-replica count of zero allows the endpoint to scale down during idle hours and back up under business-hour load.

questions.question: An MLOps team must ensure every Vertex AI Pipeline run produces auditable lineage of input datasets, transformations, and resulting model artifacts so a regulator can trace any prediction back to its training data. Which TWO mechanisms should they rely on?
options: Manually export Vertex AI Pipelines DAG definitions to Git and require pull requests for every change, since Git history is the only auditable source for regulatory traceability
Cloud Storage object hashes computed manually after each run and stored in a separate BigQuery table, since Vertex AI does not provide lineage tracking for non-tabular datasets
Vertex ML Metadata, which automatically records artifacts, executions, and lineage edges for KFP components run on Vertex AI Pipelines, including dataset and model artifact identifiers
Cloud Audit Logs for the Vertex AI API along with the Pipelines run metadata that tracks resource creation and parameters, providing tamper-evident audit records of pipeline activity
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Git history captures code changes but not runtime artifact lineage or dataset versions. Option B is wrong because Vertex AI does track artifact lineage across modalities, including non-tabular data. Option C is correct because Vertex ML Metadata records artifacts, executions, and lineage edges automatically for pipeline runs. Option D is correct because Cloud Audit Logs combined with pipeline run metadata provide tamper-evident audit trails of API actions and execution context.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Git history captures code changes but not runtime artifact lineage or dataset versions.
Option B is wrong because Vertex AI does track artifact lineage across modalities, including non-tabular data.
Option C is correct because Vertex ML Metadata records artifacts, executions, and lineage edges automatically for pipeline runs.
Option D is correct because Cloud Audit Logs combined with pipeline run metadata provide tamper-evident audit trails of API actions and execution context.

questions.question: A retail chatbot must understand multi-turn customer conversations in natural language and integrate with the website and Genesys IVR. Which Google AI API best meets this requirement?
options: Vertex AI Search with a website data store providing keyword search responses and basic ranking, surfaced through a chat widget that calls the Search API on every customer message
Conversational Agents (Dialogflow CX), which provides multi-turn natural language understanding, integrations to web and telephony channels, and stateful conversation design
Cloud Translation API combined with Cloud Natural Language entity extraction so the chatbot understands user intent based on entity coverage rather than dialog state
Speech-to-Text streaming API used standalone to transcribe customer messages, then routing transcripts to a custom-coded keyword-rules engine running on Cloud Run
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Vertex AI Search is for search and retrieval, not stateful multi-turn dialog management. Option B is correct because Conversational Agents (Dialogflow CX) is purpose-built for multi-turn dialog with stateful design and integrations to web and telephony channels including Genesys. Option C is wrong because intent inference from entity extraction alone misses dialog state. Option D is wrong because Speech-to-Text only transcribes audio and offers no language understanding or dialog management.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Vertex AI Search is for search and retrieval, not stateful multi-turn dialog management.
Option B is correct because Conversational Agents (Dialogflow CX) is purpose-built for multi-turn dialog with stateful design and integrations to web and telephony channels including Genesys.
Option C is wrong because intent inference from entity extraction alone misses dialog state.
Option D is wrong because Speech-to-Text only transcribes audio and offers no language understanding or dialog management.

questions.question: A media company needs to generate marketing imagery from text prompts, including brand-safe images suitable for a regulated advertising campaign. Which Google AI service should be used?
options: Cloud Vision API in conjunction with Cloud Storage to detect labels and faces, then build a composite image from existing brand photography by stitching labeled regions together
Cloud Translation API to translate text prompts into structured tags that downstream marketing tools convert into stock images through their own creative-asset libraries
Vertex AI Search with media-grounding so that any text prompt is mapped to the most relevant pre-existing brand asset, ensuring images are reused rather than generated newly
Vertex AI Imagen models accessed through the Model Garden, which generate brand-safe images from text prompts with SynthID watermarking and configurable safety filters
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Vision API analyzes images but does not generate them. Option B is wrong because Translation does not generate marketing imagery. Option C is wrong because Vertex AI Search retrieves existing assets and does not generate new images on demand. Option D is correct because Imagen on Vertex AI generates brand-safe images from text prompts with configurable safety filters and SynthID watermarking for compliance use cases.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Vision API analyzes images but does not generate them.
Option B is wrong because Translation does not generate marketing imagery.
Option C is wrong because Vertex AI Search retrieves existing assets and does not generate new images on demand.
Option D is correct because Imagen on Vertex AI generates brand-safe images from text prompts with configurable safety filters and SynthID watermarking for compliance use cases.

questions.question: A call-center operator needs sub-second streaming transcription of inbound calls into multiple languages with speaker diarization for analytics. Which Google AI service is most appropriate?
options: Speech-to-Text v2 streaming API with multilingual recognition and speaker diarization, delivering low-latency transcripts suitable for real-time analytics and downstream NLU systems
Cloud Translation Hub, which is the Google-recommended path for real-time multilingual call transcription with built-in diarization and speaker labels for telephony streams
Conversational Agents (Dialogflow CX), which provides streaming transcription as a side effect of its dialog handling pipeline and is preferred over Speech-to-Text for call centers
Vertex AI Search with a contact-center data store and live transcription handlers, since Search is the canonical Google API for streaming voice analytics at sub-second latency
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Speech-to-Text v2 streaming supports low-latency multilingual transcription with speaker diarization for telephony workloads. Option B is wrong because Translation Hub focuses on managed text translation, not real-time speech transcription. Option C is wrong because Dialogflow CX is for dialog management, and using it solely for transcription is the wrong abstraction. Option D is wrong because Vertex AI Search is a retrieval system, not a streaming speech transcription API.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because Speech-to-Text v2 streaming supports low-latency multilingual transcription with speaker diarization for telephony workloads.
Option B is wrong because Translation Hub focuses on managed text translation, not real-time speech transcription.
Option C is wrong because Dialogflow CX is for dialog management, and using it solely for transcription is the wrong abstraction.
Option D is wrong because Vertex AI Search is a retrieval system, not a streaming speech transcription API.

questions.question: An enterprise wants employees to chat with internal documents stored in Google Drive, Confluence, and SharePoint, with answers grounded in proprietary content, source citations, and enterprise-grade access controls. Which Google solution should the architect propose?
options: Cloud Storage backed search using BigQuery external tables and SQL prompts written by employees, since Gemini through SQL provides the strongest internal document chat experience
Gemini Enterprise (with NotebookLM and AI Agents) connected to the enterprise data sources, providing grounded chat over the user's permitted documents with citations and enterprise controls
Open-source LangChain agents deployed on GKE with custom OAuth integrations into Drive, Confluence, and SharePoint, since Google does not provide a managed enterprise chat-over-docs product
Vertex AI custom training on a copy of all documents to produce a fine-tuned model and serve it via Vertex AI endpoints, with a custom UI for employees to ask questions
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because writing SQL to chat with documents is impractical, ignores access controls, and is not the recommended pattern for enterprise document chat. Option B is correct because Gemini Enterprise (including NotebookLM and AI Agents) provides grounded enterprise chat over connected data sources with citations and access-aware retrieval. Option C is wrong because Google does offer a managed enterprise chat product, and building from scratch on GKE adds significant operational burden. Option D is wrong because fine-tuning on all documents lacks freshness, citations, and per-document access controls.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because writing SQL to chat with documents is impractical, ignores access controls, and is not the recommended pattern for enterprise document chat.
Option B is correct because Gemini Enterprise (including NotebookLM and AI Agents) provides grounded enterprise chat over connected data sources with citations and access-aware retrieval.
Option C is wrong because Google does offer a managed enterprise chat product, and building from scratch on GKE adds significant operational burden.
Option D is wrong because fine-tuning on all documents lacks freshness, citations, and per-document access controls.

questions.question: A team wants to inspect, evaluate, and deploy first-party and open-source foundation models such as Gemini, Llama, and Mistral from a single managed catalog inside Google Cloud. Which capability should they use?
options: Maintain a private Artifact Registry of model containers and write internal documentation that engineers must follow when selecting which open-source models to deploy on Vertex AI
Use Vertex AI Model Garden, which provides a curated catalog of first-party and open-source foundation models with one-click deploy, evaluation tooling, and managed serving on Vertex AI
Browse Hugging Face manually, download model weights to Cloud Storage, then build custom Vertex AI training and serving containers per model selected by the data science team
Use Cloud Marketplace's model section to install foundation models into a project, since Marketplace is the official Google Cloud catalog for first-party and third-party model artifacts
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because a private Artifact Registry does not provide evaluation, deployment, or curated catalog capabilities. Option B is correct because Vertex AI Model Garden is the curated catalog of first-party and open-source models with one-click deploy, evaluation, and managed serving. Option C is wrong because manual Hugging Face workflows discard the managed catalog, evaluation, and deployment features Model Garden provides. Option D is wrong because Cloud Marketplace is for solutions and software, not foundation-model curation.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because a private Artifact Registry does not provide evaluation, deployment, or curated catalog capabilities.
Option B is correct because Vertex AI Model Garden is the curated catalog of first-party and open-source models with one-click deploy, evaluation, and managed serving.
Option C is wrong because manual Hugging Face workflows discard the managed catalog, evaluation, and deployment features Model Garden provides.
Option D is wrong because Cloud Marketplace is for solutions and software, not foundation-model curation.

questions.question: A team needs to deploy a virtual agent that handles natural language requests, takes actions in third-party SaaS systems through APIs, and uses Gemini for reasoning. Which Google product should be used?
options: Cloud Functions, which can be wired together with HTTP triggers to call Gemini and the third-party APIs, providing the simplest path to a working virtual agent without additional platforms
Gemini Enterprise AI Agents (Agent Builder / Agentspace), which combine Gemini reasoning with declarative tools and connectors to take actions in SaaS systems on behalf of the user
Vertex AI Vision, which analyzes images and video and is the standard Google solution for building agents that interact with both customers and back-end SaaS systems
Apigee X with custom JavaScript policies that orchestrate API calls and prompt Gemini through the Vertex AI REST API on every inbound user interaction with the agent
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Cloud Functions can run code but lack the managed agent framework, tool integrations, and identity-aware connectors expected for enterprise agents. Option B is correct because Gemini Enterprise AI Agents (Agent Builder/Agentspace) combine Gemini reasoning with managed tools and connectors to take actions in SaaS systems. Option C is wrong because Vertex AI Vision is for image and video analysis, not agentic SaaS automation. Option D is wrong because Apigee X is an API management product, not an agent framework.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Cloud Functions can run code but lack the managed agent framework, tool integrations, and identity-aware connectors expected for enterprise agents.
Option B is correct because Gemini Enterprise AI Agents (Agent Builder/Agentspace) combine Gemini reasoning with managed tools and connectors to take actions in SaaS systems.
Option C is wrong because Vertex AI Vision is for image and video analysis, not agentic SaaS automation.
Option D is wrong because Apigee X is an API management product, not an agent framework.

questions.question: A team needs to ingest large training corpora that include video and audio, generate embeddings, and run grounded retrieval inside a Vertex AI pipeline. Which combination of AI APIs is most appropriate?
options: Use Video Intelligence and Speech-to-Text to extract structured signals from raw media, then call Vertex AI Embeddings to generate vector embeddings stored in Vertex AI Vector Search for retrieval
Use Cloud Translation to convert all media into English transcripts and store the transcripts directly in BigQuery, performing retrieval via SQL LIKE queries during pipeline execution
Use Vertex AI Search with a tabular data store containing the file URIs and rely on Search to extract text from media at query time, without any prior embedding generation step
Use Cloud Vision API on each video frame at training time and store the labels in Cloud Storage, then use a custom regex retrieval service for grounded retrieval at inference time
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Video Intelligence and Speech-to-Text extract structured content from video and audio, Vertex AI Embeddings generate vector representations, and Vertex AI Vector Search provides scalable grounded retrieval. Option B is wrong because Translation does not solve embeddings or retrieval, and SQL LIKE is not vector search. Option C is wrong because Vertex AI Search does not transcribe video and audio at query time as described. Option D is wrong because frame-level Vision labels lose audio context and regex retrieval is not semantic search.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because Video Intelligence and Speech-to-Text extract structured content from video and audio, Vertex AI Embeddings generate vector representations, and Vertex AI Vector Search provides scalable grounded retrieval.
Option B is wrong because Translation does not solve embeddings or retrieval, and SQL LIKE is not vector search.
Option C is wrong because Vertex AI Search does not transcribe video and audio at query time as described.
Option D is wrong because frame-level Vision labels lose audio context and regex retrieval is not semantic search.

questions.question: A platform team manages a Google Cloud organization with hundreds of projects organized under environment folders (prod, nonprod, sandbox). They want network administrators to be able to manage firewall rules in every production project without having to grant the role project-by-project as new projects are created. Which approach should the architect recommend?
options: Grant the Compute Security Admin role to the network admins group at the prod folder level so the binding inherits to every current and future project beneath it
Create a custom role at the organization that includes compute.firewalls.update and assign it directly to each project as projects are provisioned by the central platform team
Add the network admins group to the Owner role on each production project so they have full administrative authority over firewall configurations and any other resource
Grant the Compute Security Admin role at the organization node and rely on organization policies to scope the binding to production projects at runtime evaluation
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because IAM bindings on a folder are inherited by all projects under that folder, so granting Compute Security Admin at the prod folder automatically applies to current and future projects without per-project administration. Option B is wrong because requiring a per-project binding defeats the purpose of the resource hierarchy and creates manual toil every time a project is created. Option C is wrong because Owner grants far more than firewall management, violating least privilege and separation of duties for a network team. Option D is wrong because organization policies do not scope IAM bindings to a subset of projects; they constrain resource configuration, not principal-to-role grants.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because IAM bindings on a folder are inherited by all projects under that folder, so granting Compute Security Admin at the prod folder automatically applies to current and future projects without per-project administration.
Option B is wrong because requiring a per-project binding defeats the purpose of the resource hierarchy and creates manual toil every time a project is created.
Option C is wrong because Owner grants far more than firewall management, violating least privilege and separation of duties for a network team.
Option D is wrong because organization policies do not scope IAM bindings to a subset of projects; they constrain resource configuration, not principal-to-role grants.

questions.question: A security team needs to ensure that no service account key files can be created anywhere in the organization, in order to push teams toward Workload Identity Federation and service account impersonation. Which control should they apply?
options: Add a deny IAM policy at the organization node that denies iam.serviceAccountKeys.create permission for all principals across the resource hierarchy by default
Enforce the iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation organization policy constraint at the organization node so key creation is blocked at every project
Configure VPC Service Controls to add the IAM API to a service perimeter so calls to create service account keys are blocked at the network edge
Audit all projects daily with a Cloud Asset Inventory feed and rotate or delete any newly created service account keys older than 24 hours automatically
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because while IAM deny policies are powerful, the canonical and recommended control for this exact scenario is the dedicated organization policy constraint, which is purpose-built and enforced by every project. Option B is correct because iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation is the organization policy constraint specifically designed to block creation of user-managed service account keys across the hierarchy. Option C is wrong because VPC Service Controls protect data exfiltration across a perimeter — they do not prevent administrative actions like creating an IAM credential. Option D is wrong because detect-and-delete is reactive and leaves a window of exposure; a preventive policy is the correct posture.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because while IAM deny policies are powerful, the canonical and recommended control for this exact scenario is the dedicated organization policy constraint, which is purpose-built and enforced by every project.

Option B is correct because iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation is the organization policy constraint specifically designed to block creation of user-managed service account keys across the hierarchy.
Option C is wrong because VPC Service Controls protect data exfiltration across a perimeter — they do not prevent administrative actions like creating an IAM credential.
Option D is wrong because detect-and-delete is reactive and leaves a window of exposure; a preventive policy is the correct posture.

questions.question: An external SaaS partner needs to read objects from a single Cloud Storage bucket once per hour. The architect wants to avoid distributing long-lived service account keys to the partner. Which approach best meets the requirement?
options: Configure Workload Identity Federation with the partner's OIDC identity provider and grant the federated principal the Storage Object Viewer role on the target bucket only
Create a dedicated service account, download a JSON key, encrypt it with a customer-managed key, and email the encrypted key to the partner along with the decryption passphrase
Grant the partner's personal Google account the Storage Object Viewer role on the project and have them run gsutil with their user credentials during the scheduled hourly job
Make the bucket public with uniform bucket-level access disabled and rely on object name obfuscation so only the partner who has the URL can retrieve the objects
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Workload Identity Federation lets external workloads exchange their existing OIDC tokens for short-lived Google Cloud credentials without any long-lived service account key being distributed. Option B is wrong because emailing a JSON key — even encrypted — reintroduces the long-lived credential the design is trying to avoid and creates a key-distribution problem. Option C is wrong because using a personal Google account ties access to an individual rather than a workload and breaks when the person leaves the partner organization. Option D is wrong because security through obscurity is not a control; a publicly readable bucket exposes data to anyone who guesses or leaks the URL.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because Workload Identity Federation lets external workloads exchange their existing OIDC tokens for short-lived Google Cloud credentials without any long-lived service account key being distributed.
Option B is wrong because emailing a JSON key — even encrypted — reintroduces the long-lived credential the design is trying to avoid and creates a key-distribution problem.
Option C is wrong because using a personal Google account ties access to an individual rather than a workload and breaks when the person leaves the partner organization.
Option D is wrong because security through obscurity is not a control; a publicly readable bucket exposes data to anyone who guesses or leaks the URL.

questions.question: A regulated bank wants data engineers to be able to submit BigQuery jobs against a sensitive dataset only when connecting from a corporate-managed device that has passed posture checks. Which TWO controls should the architect combine? (Choose two.)
options: Place the BigQuery dataset in a VPC Service Controls perimeter and rely on the perimeter alone to enforce device-based access requirements on every incoming call
Configure Chrome Enterprise Premium with an access level that requires a managed device with up-to-date OS patches and screen lock enabled before any policy can apply
Bind the access level to the BigQuery API using Context-Aware Access so unmanaged devices are denied even with valid credentials presented at the API layer
Grant the data engineers the BigQuery Job User role only on weekdays using a Cloud Function that rotates IAM bindings every Monday morning at the start of business
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because VPC Service Controls enforce network and identity perimeters but do not by themselves evaluate client device posture, which is what the requirement demands. Option B is correct because Chrome Enterprise Premium provides access levels evaluated from device posture signals such as managed status, OS patch level, and screen lock — the building block for device-aware access. Option C is correct because Context-Aware Access binds those access levels to specific Google Cloud APIs, so the BigQuery API will reject requests from devices that fail the posture check even when the user has valid IAM. Option D is wrong because rotating IAM bindings on a schedule is brittle, has no relation to device posture, and creates outages for legitimate weekend work.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because VPC Service Controls enforce network and identity perimeters but do not by themselves evaluate client device posture, which is what the requirement demands.

Option B is correct because Chrome Enterprise Premium provides access levels evaluated from device posture signals such as managed status, OS patch level, and screen lock — the building block for device-aware access.

Option C is correct because Context-Aware Access binds those access levels to specific Google Cloud APIs, so the BigQuery API will reject requests from devices that fail the posture check even when the user has valid IAM.
Option D is wrong because rotating IAM bindings on a schedule is brittle, has no relation to device posture, and creates outages for legitimate weekend work.

questions.question: An architect is designing CMEK protection for a Cloud Storage bucket holding PII. The CISO requires that key material never exists in software form and is generated and stored only inside FIPS 140-2 Level 3 hardware. Which Cloud KMS configuration meets the requirement?
options: Create a Cloud KMS key with protection level SOFTWARE and rotate the key automatically every 30 days to compensate for the lack of hardware backing
Use Google-managed encryption keys for the bucket and document in the runbook that Google holds the hardware modules on the customer's behalf
Create a Cloud KMS key with protection level HSM so the key is generated and used inside a FIPS 140-2 Level 3 hardware security module
Import a customer-supplied encryption key (CSEK) as a raw 256-bit value into the application configuration and supply it on every Cloud Storage request
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because SOFTWARE protection level means the key material exists as a protected blob in software, which fails the CISO's hardware-only requirement regardless of rotation cadence. Option B is wrong because Google-managed keys are not customer-managed at all — the customer cannot prove FIPS 140-2 Level 3 hardware backing, and the requirement is for CMEK. Option C is correct because Cloud KMS HSM protection level guarantees that key generation, storage, and cryptographic operations occur inside a FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated hardware security module. Option D is wrong because customer-supplied encryption keys are raw values supplied by the customer per request; they are not stored in an HSM and are typically held in software by the customer.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because SOFTWARE protection level means the key material exists as a protected blob in software, which fails the CISO's hardware-only requirement regardless of rotation cadence.
Option B is wrong because Google-managed keys are not customer-managed at all — the customer cannot prove FIPS 140-2 Level 3 hardware backing, and the requirement is for CMEK.
Option C is correct because Cloud KMS HSM protection level guarantees that key generation, storage, and cryptographic operations occur inside a FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated hardware security module.
Option D is wrong because customer-supplied encryption keys are raw values supplied by the customer per request; they are not stored in an HSM and are typically held in software by the customer.

questions.question: A platform team wants to make sure that even a project owner cannot delete the production Cloud Storage bucket holding regulatory records. The bucket must remain in place for seven years under a retention policy. Which combination of controls best protects the records?
options: Apply an organization policy denying storage.buckets.delete and trust that future owners will not request an exception during the seven-year window
Grant the Storage Object Viewer role to project owners and remove all higher-privileged roles from production so deletes simply fail with a permission error
Move the bucket to a separate folder owned by the security team only and instruct application teams to use signed URLs whenever they need access
Apply a Bucket Lock with a 7-year retention policy so objects cannot be deleted before the period ends and the policy itself cannot be shortened
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because organization policies can be overridden by a sufficiently privileged administrator at the org node, and 'trust' is not a control. Option B is wrong because stripping owners of their role to enforce a single rule creates massive operational problems and does not protect against re-granting the role. Option C is wrong because moving the bucket changes ownership but does not enforce retention; signed URLs are an access pattern, not an immutability control. Option D is correct because Bucket Lock makes the retention policy immutable and prevents object deletion before the retention period ends, providing WORM-style records protection that even project owners cannot bypass.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because organization policies can be overridden by a sufficiently privileged administrator at the org node, and 'trust' is not a control.
Option B is wrong because stripping owners of their role to enforce a single rule creates massive operational problems and does not protect against re-granting the role.
Option C is wrong because moving the bucket changes ownership but does not enforce retention; signed URLs are an access pattern, not an immutability control.

Option D is correct because Bucket Lock makes the retention policy immutable and prevents object deletion before the retention period ends, providing WORM-style records protection that even project owners cannot bypass.

questions.question: A developer needs to run a one-time data migration that requires permissions only the SRE team currently holds. The security team wants to avoid permanently granting elevated roles to the developer. Which Google Cloud capability should the architect recommend?
options: Configure service account impersonation so the developer's user account can mint short-lived tokens for a privileged service account only when explicitly invoking the migration
Add the developer to the SRE group for the duration of the migration and remove them afterward, relying on the team lead to remember to revoke the access
Share an SRE engineer's user credentials with the developer so they can run the migration as the SRE engineer and the work is attributed to the engineer's identity
Grant the developer the Owner role on the project for one week and rely on Cloud Audit Logs to detect any actions outside the scope of the migration
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because service account impersonation lets a user request short-lived credentials for a service account that holds the elevated permissions, providing scoped, auditable, just-in-time access without permanently granting roles to the user. Option B is wrong because manual group membership management is error-prone, depends on humans remembering to revoke, and offers no per-action attribution. Option C is wrong because sharing user credentials breaks attribution, violates Google Cloud terms, and creates an account-takeover risk. Option D is wrong because granting Owner is wildly over-privileged for a single migration and relies on detective rather than preventive controls.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because service account impersonation lets a user request short-lived credentials for a service account that holds the elevated permissions, providing scoped, auditable, just-in-time access without permanently granting roles to the user.
Option B is wrong because manual group membership management is error-prone, depends on humans remembering to revoke, and offers no per-action attribution.
Option C is wrong because sharing user credentials breaks attribution, violates Google Cloud terms, and creates an account-takeover risk.
Option D is wrong because granting Owner is wildly over-privileged for a single migration and relies on detective rather than preventive controls.

questions.question: A retailer wants to detect and redact credit card numbers and government IDs from customer chat transcripts before storing them in BigQuery. The pipeline runs on Dataflow. Which managed service should the architect use?
options: Sensitive Data Protection (formerly Cloud DLP) with inspection and de-identification templates invoked from the Dataflow pipeline to redact or tokenize sensitive findings
Cloud Armor with preconfigured WAF rules that scan request payloads and block any request that contains a credit card number pattern before it reaches the pipeline
Binary Authorization with custom attestations that prevent the Dataflow worker image from running unless it has been scanned for sensitive data leakage at build time
Cloud Key Management Service with rotating symmetric keys to encrypt all transcript fields and decrypt them on demand inside BigQuery views
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Sensitive Data Protection is purpose-built for discovering and de-identifying sensitive elements like credit card numbers and government IDs, and integrates with Dataflow via templates and transforms. Option B is wrong because Cloud Armor protects HTTP load balancers from web attacks; it does not inspect or redact PII inside data being processed downstream. Option C is wrong because Binary Authorization controls which container images may be deployed; it does not inspect data content. Option D is wrong because encrypting whole fields blocks downstream analytics and does not selectively redact only the sensitive substrings inside a transcript.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because Sensitive Data Protection is purpose-built for discovering and de-identifying sensitive elements like credit card numbers and government IDs, and integrates with Dataflow via templates and transforms.
Option B is wrong because Cloud Armor protects HTTP load balancers from web attacks; it does not inspect or redact PII inside data being processed downstream.
Option C is wrong because Binary Authorization controls which container images may be deployed; it does not inspect data content.
Option D is wrong because encrypting whole fields blocks downstream analytics and does not selectively redact only the sensitive substrings inside a transcript.

questions.question: An organization has multiple folders and projects, and the security team wants to ensure that Compute Engine VMs in the entire organization can never be created with public IP addresses, regardless of who provisions them. Which approach is correct?
options: Manually delete any VM that is created with a public IP using a Cloud Function triggered by Cloud Audit Logs and notify the project owner each time it fires
Add a firewall rule at each VPC that denies all egress traffic from public IPs, indirectly making public IPs useless and discouraging their creation
Configure Cloud NAT in every VPC so that VMs with public IPs are automatically given private routing, removing the need for external addresses
Set the constraints/compute.vmExternalIpAccess organization policy at the organization node to deny external IPs on all VMs in the hierarchy
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because reactive deletion leaves an exposure window and does not prevent the creation in the first place; a preventive policy is required. Option B is wrong because egress firewall rules on the VPC do not control whether VMs are assigned external IPs at provisioning time. Option C is wrong because Cloud NAT provides outbound translation for private VMs, but it does not prevent administrators from still creating VMs with external IPs attached. Option D is correct because the compute.vmExternalIpAccess organization policy constraint preventively denies external IP assignment to VMs across the hierarchy and is the canonical control for this requirement.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because reactive deletion leaves an exposure window and does not prevent the creation in the first place; a preventive policy is required.
Option B is wrong because egress firewall rules on the VPC do not control whether VMs are assigned external IPs at provisioning time.
Option C is wrong because Cloud NAT provides outbound translation for private VMs, but it does not prevent administrators from still creating VMs with external IPs attached.

Option D is correct because the compute.vmExternalIpAccess organization policy constraint preventively denies external IP assignment to VMs across the hierarchy and is the canonical control for this requirement.

questions.question: A security architect must ensure that data exfiltration from a confidential BigQuery dataset is blocked even when an attacker compromises a project Editor's credentials. The dataset must remain accessible to legitimate workloads running in two specific projects. Which control should be applied?
options: Switch all BigQuery service accounts to use customer-supplied encryption keys (CSEK) so a stolen credential cannot decrypt data outside the original projects
Apply a Cloud KMS rotation schedule of every 24 hours so any credential that an attacker steals becomes useless within a day of compromise
Set the BigQuery dataset to use Authorized Views only and require all queries to go through a single approved view in a shared analytics project for monitoring
Place the BigQuery project in a VPC Service Controls perimeter that allows ingress only from the two specified projects so calls from any other project or network are denied
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because BigQuery does not support CSEK for dataset encryption (only CMEK), and even CMEK does not prevent an authorized credential from reading data — it changes who controls the key. Option B is wrong because Cloud KMS rotation does not invalidate already-issued OAuth tokens an attacker may hold, and short rotation is operationally disruptive. Option C is wrong because Authorized Views are a data-sharing pattern; they do not stop an Editor with the credential from querying the source dataset directly. Option D is correct because VPC Service Controls create a security perimeter that blocks API access from outside the perimeter even with valid credentials, neutralizing stolen-credential exfiltration paths.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because BigQuery does not support CSEK for dataset encryption (only CMEK), and even CMEK does not prevent an authorized credential from reading data — it changes who controls the key.
Option B is wrong because Cloud KMS rotation does not invalidate already-issued OAuth tokens an attacker may hold, and short rotation is operationally disruptive.
Option C is wrong because Authorized Views are a data-sharing pattern; they do not stop an Editor with the credential from querying the source dataset directly.

Option D is correct because VPC Service Controls create a security perimeter that blocks API access from outside the perimeter even with valid credentials, neutralizing stolen-credential exfiltration paths.

questions.question: A team uses Cloud Build to ship containers to GKE. Compliance requires that only images that have passed vulnerability scanning AND have been signed by the QA team's attestor can be deployed to the production cluster. Which TWO Google Cloud features together meet this requirement? (Choose two.)
options: Container-Optimized OS for nodes plus a workload identity pool restricted to the QA service account so only QA-built images can authenticate to pull from the registry
Artifact Analysis vulnerability scanning that produces signed scan results an attestor consumes for each container image built and pushed by the pipeline
Binary Authorization policies on the production cluster that require attestations from both a vulnerability-scan attestor and the QA attestor before admission
Cloud KMS image signing with an HSM-backed key combined with VPC Service Controls around the GKE cluster to reject any pull from outside the production perimeter
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Container-Optimized OS hardens the node OS but does not enforce image signing or vulnerability gating policies, and identity pool restrictions do not enforce content attestation. Option B is correct because Artifact Analysis produces vulnerability scan results that an attestor can sign, providing the proof that a scan was completed for each image. Option C is correct because Binary Authorization enforces at GKE admission that required attestations (scan + QA) are present before allowing the workload to run. Option D is wrong because Cloud KMS can sign artifacts but VPC Service Controls do not enforce attestation policy on container images at the cluster admission level.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Container-Optimized OS hardens the node OS but does not enforce image signing or vulnerability gating policies, and identity pool restrictions do not enforce content attestation.
Option B is correct because Artifact Analysis produces vulnerability scan results that an attestor can sign, providing the proof that a scan was completed for each image.
Option C is correct because Binary Authorization enforces at GKE admission that required attestations (scan + QA) are present before allowing the workload to run.
Option D is wrong because Cloud KMS can sign artifacts but VPC Service Controls do not enforce attestation policy on container images at the cluster admission level.

questions.question: A startup launching a generative AI assistant on Vertex AI needs to enforce safety policies that block prompt injection attempts and reject responses containing harmful content categories before they reach end users. Which Google Cloud product addresses this?
options: Cloud Armor with adaptive protection rules configured to inspect JSON payloads and drop requests that match known prompt-injection signatures the security team collects
Model Armor, which provides safety policies that screen prompts for injection attempts and responses for harmful content before they are delivered to end users
Sensitive Data Protection inspection templates running synchronously on every model response to find and redact unsafe categories before the response is returned
Cloud IDS configured in passive mode at the regional load balancer so suspicious prompt patterns are detected and forwarded to Chronicle for analyst review
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Cloud Armor protects HTTP(S) load balancers from web attacks like DDoS and SQLi; it is not designed to evaluate LLM prompt safety or response content. Option B is correct because Model Armor is the Google Cloud service designed specifically to apply safety policies to LLM prompts and responses, including prompt-injection screening and harmful-content category enforcement. Option C is wrong because Sensitive Data Protection is excellent at finding regulated data like PII but is not built around the categories of LLM safety (jailbreaks, harmful content classification, prompt injection). Option D is wrong because Cloud IDS is a network intrusion detection product; it is not designed for inspecting LLM application semantics.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Cloud Armor protects HTTP(S) load balancers from web attacks like DDoS and SQLi; it is not designed to evaluate LLM prompt safety or response content.

Option B is correct because Model Armor is the Google Cloud service designed specifically to apply safety policies to LLM prompts and responses, including prompt-injection screening and harmful-content category enforcement.

Option C is wrong because Sensitive Data Protection is excellent at finding regulated data like PII but is not built around the categories of LLM safety (jailbreaks, harmful content classification, prompt injection).
Option D is wrong because Cloud IDS is a network intrusion detection product; it is not designed for inspecting LLM application semantics.

questions.question: A healthcare SaaS company storing electronic health records on Google Cloud must comply with HIPAA. Which combination of controls is most appropriate?
options: Disable Cloud Audit Logs to avoid logging PHI accidentally, encrypt with Google-managed keys, and rely on the BAA to cover the company's processing of records
Move all PHI to an on-premises data center and use Google Cloud only for non-PHI analytics, since Google Cloud cannot legally process protected health information
Use Google-managed encryption keys, enable Admin Activity logs only since they cover PHI access, and add VPC Service Controls to block all external traffic
Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with Google, use only HIPAA-eligible services, enable Data Access audit logs, and apply CMEK for encryption control
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because disabling audit logs is not how HIPAA compliance works — HIPAA requires logging access to PHI, and Data Access logs are central to that. Option B is wrong because Google Cloud has many HIPAA-eligible services and is widely used by healthcare customers under BAAs. Option C is wrong because Admin Activity logs do not capture PHI read events — Data Access logs do — and Google-managed keys are not customer-controlled. Option D is correct because the canonical HIPAA path on Google Cloud is: execute a BAA, restrict workloads to HIPAA-eligible services, enable Data Access audit logs to track PHI access, and use CMEK so the customer controls the encryption keys protecting PHI.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because disabling audit logs is not how HIPAA compliance works — HIPAA requires logging access to PHI, and Data Access logs are central to that.
Option B is wrong because Google Cloud has many HIPAA-eligible services and is widely used by healthcare customers under BAAs.
Option C is wrong because Admin Activity logs do not capture PHI read events — Data Access logs do — and Google-managed keys are not customer-controlled.

Option D is correct because the canonical HIPAA path on Google Cloud is: execute a BAA, restrict workloads to HIPAA-eligible services, enable Data Access audit logs to track PHI access, and use CMEK so the customer controls the encryption keys protecting PHI.

questions.question: An auditor needs to verify exactly who read records from a particular BigQuery dataset over the last 90 days. By default, which Cloud Audit Log type captures this information, and what must the team check?
options: Admin Activity logs are always on and automatically capture every BigQuery query and read; the auditor only needs to filter by dataset name to retrieve the events
System Event logs include data-plane reads on BigQuery because BigQuery is a system service; no configuration change is required to satisfy the auditor
Policy Denied logs capture every successful and denied data read; the team should review the Policy Denied stream for the project to find the BigQuery activity
Data Access logs (specifically the DATA_READ subtype) capture reads; they are disabled by default for most services and must be explicitly enabled for BigQuery before the events exist
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Admin Activity logs capture administrative writes (create, update, delete) — not the data-plane read events the auditor needs. Option B is wrong because System Event logs record Google-Cloud-initiated administrative actions on resources, not user data reads against BigQuery. Option C is wrong because Policy Denied logs record requests denied by VPC Service Controls or IAM deny policies, not successful read activity. Option D is correct because Data Access logs include DATA_READ events and are off by default for most services; they must be explicitly configured to be captured and retained for an audit to find them.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Admin Activity logs capture administrative writes (create, update, delete) — not the data-plane read events the auditor needs.
Option B is wrong because System Event logs record Google-Cloud-initiated administrative actions on resources, not user data reads against BigQuery.
Option C is wrong because Policy Denied logs record requests denied by VPC Service Controls or IAM deny policies, not successful read activity.

Option D is correct because Data Access logs include DATA_READ events and are off by default for most services; they must be explicitly configured to be captured and retained for an audit to find them.

questions.question: A multinational company needs to ensure that customer data from EU users remains stored only in European regions to satisfy GDPR data residency expectations. Which Google Cloud control most directly enforces this?
options: Apply CMEK with key rings in europe-west4 so the encryption itself anchors data to the European region regardless of where storage buckets or BigQuery datasets are created
Enforce the constraints/gcp.resourceLocations organization policy to allow only European location values when creating storage buckets, BigQuery datasets, and other location-aware resources
Configure Cloud DNS to resolve storage endpoints only to European IPs so any attempt to create a bucket outside Europe is silently routed back to a European region
Tag all EU customer projects with a customer:eu label and create a Cloud Function that deletes resources in non-European regions every hour for projects with that label
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the location of a KMS key does not constrain where the encrypted data is stored — a bucket in us-central1 can still use a key from europe-west4. Option B is correct because the gcp.resourceLocations organization policy constraint preventively limits the regions and multi-regions where location-aware resources may be created, which is the canonical Google Cloud data-residency control. Option C is wrong because Cloud DNS does not influence where resources are provisioned; resource location is a property set at creation time via the API. Option D is wrong because reactive deletion creates exposure windows and operational risk, and it depends on labels being applied correctly.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because the location of a KMS key does not constrain where the encrypted data is stored — a bucket in us-central1 can still use a key from europe-west4.

Option B is correct because the gcp.resourceLocations organization policy constraint preventively limits the regions and multi-regions where location-aware resources may be created, which is the canonical Google Cloud data-residency control.
Option C is wrong because Cloud DNS does not influence where resources are provisioned; resource location is a property set at creation time via the API.
Option D is wrong because reactive deletion creates exposure windows and operational risk, and it depends on labels being applied correctly.
