Option C is wrong because Apache Bench is a single-host tool with no native distributed coordination and cannot easily reach the target rate or expose Prometheus metrics. Option D is correct because Locust runs in distributed master/worker mode on GKE, the workload is defined in a Python tasks file (code), workers scale horizontally to reach the target RPS, and Locust exposes Prometheus-compatible metrics for monitoring.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because curl loops from laptops will not generate 50,000 RPS and have no centralized metrics.
Option B is wrong because a single VM, especially using the GUI, cannot reliably sustain 50,000 RPS and is not horizontally scalable.
Option C is wrong because Apache Bench is a single-host tool with no native distributed coordination and cannot easily reach the target rate or expose Prometheus metrics.

Option D is correct because Locust runs in distributed master/worker mode on GKE, the workload is defined in a Python tasks file (code), workers scale horizontally to reach the target RPS, and Locust exposes Prometheus-compatible metrics for monitoring.

questions.question: Two teams own services that communicate over HTTP. They want to prevent breaking changes by verifying, in CI, that the provider's API still satisfies the consumer's expectations. Which testing approach should you recommend?
options: Contract testing using a tool such as Pact, with consumer-generated pacts verified against the provider in CI
End-to-end integration tests run nightly across both services in a shared staging project
Unit tests on each service that mock the other service's responses based on the current spec
Load testing weekly to catch regressions through response-time changes during peak hours
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because contract testing (for example with Pact) is purpose-built for this: the consumer generates a contract describing the requests it issues and responses it expects, and the provider verifies that contract in its CI pipeline, catching breaking changes before deploy. Option B is wrong because nightly end-to-end tests catch issues late, are slow, and indicate failure without isolating the contract change. Option C is wrong because mock-based unit tests can drift from the real provider behavior since mocks are not verified against the actual API. Option D is wrong because load tests measure performance, not API correctness, and would not reliably detect a breaking schema change.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because contract testing (for example with Pact) is purpose-built for this: the consumer generates a contract describing the requests it issues and responses it expects, and the provider verifies that contract in its CI pipeline, catching breaking changes before deploy.
Option B is wrong because nightly end-to-end tests catch issues late, are slow, and indicate failure without isolating the contract change.
Option C is wrong because mock-based unit tests can drift from the real provider behavior since mocks are not verified against the actual API.
Option D is wrong because load tests measure performance, not API correctness, and would not reliably detect a breaking schema change.

questions.question: A team needs to copy 80 TB of files from an on-premises NFS server to Cloud Storage. The site has a 1 Gbps internet uplink that is 60% utilized by business traffic during the day. They want to complete the transfer in under two weeks with minimal disruption. Which solution should you recommend?
options: Storage Transfer Service with an on-premises agent, scheduling transfers to off-peak hours with bandwidth caps
Run gsutil cp -m from a single workstation continuously and rely on the network team to deprioritize other traffic
Order a Transfer Appliance, copy data to it locally, and ship it back to Google for ingest
Mount the NFS share over Cloud VPN and copy files using a single rsync job on a Compute Engine VM
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Storage Transfer Service for on-premises data deploys agents at the source, supports scheduled windows and bandwidth limits, retries on failure, and keeps a manifest of completed objects; 80 TB over 400 Mbps off-peak is comfortably under two weeks. Option B is wrong because gsutil cp from a single workstation lacks parallel agents, bandwidth control, and resumable scheduling, and asking network teams to deprioritize business traffic is disruptive. Option C is wrong because Transfer Appliance is best for very large volumes or poor connectivity; here, online transfer is fast enough and avoids shipping delays. Option D is wrong because a single rsync job over VPN is bandwidth-constrained, single-threaded, and has no scheduled bandwidth controls.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because Storage Transfer Service for on-premises data deploys agents at the source, supports scheduled windows and bandwidth limits, retries on failure, and keeps a manifest of completed objects; 80 TB over 400 Mbps off-peak is comfortably under two weeks.

Option B is wrong because gsutil cp from a single workstation lacks parallel agents, bandwidth control, and resumable scheduling, and asking network teams to deprioritize business traffic is disruptive.
Option C is wrong because Transfer Appliance is best for very large volumes or poor connectivity; here, online transfer is fast enough and avoids shipping delays.
Option D is wrong because a single rsync job over VPN is bandwidth-constrained, single-threaded, and has no scheduled bandwidth controls.

questions.question: Which command transfers a 200 GB object to Cloud Storage with the highest throughput on a modern GKE-attached Compute Engine VM that has been authenticated with gcloud?
options: gcloud storage cp ./big.tar gs://bucket/big.tar
gsutil cp ./big.tar gs://bucket/big.tar
curl -X POST with a resumable upload session URL produced from the JSON API
A custom Python script that opens the file and writes chunks one at a time using the storage client
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because gcloud storage is the new, recommended Cloud Storage CLI; it uses parallel composite uploads and CRC32C hardware acceleration by default and is significantly faster than gsutil on the same hardware. Option B is wrong because gsutil still works but is older and slower than gcloud storage; without explicit -o parallel_composite_upload_threshold tuning it underperforms. Option C is wrong because a hand-rolled curl resumable upload is single-stream and lacks the parallelism and integrity-check optimizations baked into gcloud storage. Option D is wrong because a naive single-threaded chunked Python script will be CPU- and latency-bound and cannot match the optimized parallel uploads gcloud storage performs.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because gcloud storage is the new, recommended Cloud Storage CLI; it uses parallel composite uploads and CRC32C hardware acceleration by default and is significantly faster than gsutil on the same hardware.
Option B is wrong because gsutil still works but is older and slower than gcloud storage; without explicit -o parallel_composite_upload_threshold tuning it underperforms.
Option C is wrong because a hand-rolled curl resumable upload is single-stream and lacks the parallelism and integrity-check optimizations baked into gcloud storage.
Option D is wrong because a naive single-threaded chunked Python script will be CPU- and latency-bound and cannot match the optimized parallel uploads gcloud storage performs.

questions.question: An Apigee X proxy fronts a backend service. The product manager requires that one partner be allowed at most 1,000 calls per minute, with bursts of 100 calls per second smoothed out, and excess requests rejected with HTTP 429. Which combination of Apigee policies achieves this?
options: Apply only OAuthV2 token verification on the proxy and rely on the backend service to drop excess traffic at the application layer
Apply ResponseCache and FlowCallout policies on the proxy to absorb the peak partner traffic in shared in-memory caches
Apply ExtractVariables and AssignMessage policies to add a custom header used for tracking, while enforcing every actual limit inside the backend code
Apply a Quota policy keyed on the API key for the per-minute limit, plus SpikeArrest for the per-second burst smoothing
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because OAuthV2 verifies tokens but does not enforce per-partner request limits; pushing rate enforcement to the backend defeats the purpose of API management at the edge. Option B is wrong because ResponseCache returns cached responses and FlowCallout invokes shared flows; neither enforces rate limits. Option C is wrong because tracking headers do not enforce limits, and pushing enforcement to backend code is exactly what API management policies replace. Option D is correct because Quota enforces a count over a time window (here 1,000 per minute, keyed on the API key), and SpikeArrest smooths short-term bursts to a steady allowed rate (here 100 per second); together they meet both requirements and Apigee returns HTTP 429 when limits are exceeded.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because OAuthV2 verifies tokens but does not enforce per-partner request limits; pushing rate enforcement to the backend defeats the purpose of API management at the edge.
Option B is wrong because ResponseCache returns cached responses and FlowCallout invokes shared flows; neither enforces rate limits.
Option C is wrong because tracking headers do not enforce limits, and pushing enforcement to backend code is exactly what API management policies replace.

Option D is correct because Quota enforces a count over a time window (here 1,000 per minute, keyed on the API key), and SpikeArrest smooths short-term bursts to a steady allowed rate (here 100 per second); together they meet both requirements and Apigee returns HTTP 429 when limits are exceeded.

questions.question: A retail company runs Apigee on-prem today and wants to keep some API proxies on customer-managed Kubernetes (for low-latency local calls) while moving the management plane to Google Cloud. Which Apigee deployment model fits this requirement?
options: Apigee Edge for Public Cloud, the legacy SaaS offering Google still actively sells to new customers
Apigee X with all runtime planes hosted exclusively in Google Cloud regions
Apigee Edge Microgateway deployed as a sidecar in every application pod with no central plane
Apigee hybrid, where the management plane runs in Google Cloud and the runtime plane runs in the customer's Kubernetes cluster
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Apigee Edge for Public Cloud is the legacy product; Google's current offering for new customers is Apigee X (cloud-hosted) or Apigee hybrid. Option B is wrong because Apigee X runs all runtime in Google Cloud regions, which does not meet the requirement to keep some runtime on customer-managed Kubernetes. Option C is wrong because Edge Microgateway is a legacy sidecar gateway and is not the modern model for split runtime/management; it also lacks a central management plane in Google Cloud. Option D is correct because Apigee hybrid is specifically designed for this split: the management plane (analytics, config) is in Google Cloud while the runtime plane (message processors) runs in the customer's Kubernetes cluster.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Apigee Edge for Public Cloud is the legacy product; Google's current offering for new customers is Apigee X (cloud-hosted) or Apigee hybrid.
Option B is wrong because Apigee X runs all runtime in Google Cloud regions, which does not meet the requirement to keep some runtime on customer-managed Kubernetes.
Option C is wrong because Edge Microgateway is a legacy sidecar gateway and is not the modern model for split runtime/management; it also lacks a central management plane in Google Cloud.

Option D is correct because Apigee hybrid is specifically designed for this split: the management plane (analytics, config) is in Google Cloud while the runtime plane (message processors) runs in the customer's Kubernetes cluster.

questions.question: A team accesses Google Cloud APIs from a backend running on Compute Engine. They want the strongest, most maintainable authentication pattern that requires no key files on disk. Which approach should you recommend?
options: Generate a service-account JSON key, store it on the VM, and set GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS to its path
Use a user OAuth client ID flow on first boot and cache the refresh token in /etc
Attach a service account to the VM and use Application Default Credentials, which obtains tokens from the metadata server
Embed an API key in the application config and rotate it manually every 90 days
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because service-account JSON keys on disk are a known credential-leakage risk; Google recommends avoiding them when an attached service account works. Option B is wrong because user OAuth flows tie a backend to an individual person's identity and break when that user leaves; refresh tokens on disk are also a leak risk. Option C is correct because attaching a service account to the VM and using Application Default Credentials (ADC) lets client libraries obtain short-lived tokens from the metadata server automatically; no keys land on disk and tokens rotate continuously. Option D is wrong because API keys do not represent an identity, do not grant most IAM permissions, and are appropriate only for unauthenticated quotas on public APIs.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because service-account JSON keys on disk are a known credential-leakage risk; Google recommends avoiding them when an attached service account works.
Option B is wrong because user OAuth flows tie a backend to an individual person's identity and break when that user leaves; refresh tokens on disk are also a leak risk.

Option C is correct because attaching a service account to the VM and using Application Default Credentials (ADC) lets client libraries obtain short-lived tokens from the metadata server automatically; no keys land on disk and tokens rotate continuously.
Option D is wrong because API keys do not represent an identity, do not grant most IAM permissions, and are appropriate only for unauthenticated quotas on public APIs.

questions.question: A team is calling a Google Cloud API from a backend service that occasionally observes 503 Service Unavailable responses. They want a robust client-side retry strategy that respects server signals and avoids thundering-herd retries. Which approach should you recommend?
options: Retry every failure immediately up to 10 times so that transient errors and even non-retriable errors are masked entirely from upstream callers
Retry only 5xx responses on the next fixed 1-second interval across all clients in order to keep retry timing strictly predictable
Retry retriable status codes (429, 500, 503) with exponential backoff and jitter, honoring Retry-After when set
Never retry from the client; surface every error to the caller and let the user trigger a manual retry from the UI on every transient failure
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because immediate aggressive retries amplify load on a struggling service and cause thundering-herd behavior; they also retry non-idempotent failures that should not be retried. Option B is wrong because a fixed interval still synchronizes retries across clients and ignores Retry-After hints sent by the server. Option C is correct because Google's API best practices specify retrying only retriable status codes (such as 429, 500, 503) with exponential backoff plus jitter to de-synchronize clients, and honoring the Retry-After header when the server provides one. Option D is wrong because pushing every transient error to the user produces poor UX and ignores legitimate retry semantics for idempotent calls.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because immediate aggressive retries amplify load on a struggling service and cause thundering-herd behavior; they also retry non-idempotent failures that should not be retried.
Option B is wrong because a fixed interval still synchronizes retries across clients and ignores Retry-After hints sent by the server.

Option C is correct because Google's API best practices specify retrying only retriable status codes (such as 429, 500, 503) with exponential backoff plus jitter to de-synchronize clients, and honoring the Retry-After header when the server provides one.
Option D is wrong because pushing every transient error to the user produces poor UX and ignores legitimate retry semantics for idempotent calls.

questions.question: A platform team is choosing between Terraform and Config Connector for managing Google Cloud resources from a GKE-based developer platform. Which statements correctly describe the trade-offs? (Choose two.)
options: Config Connector is the legacy successor to Deployment Manager and is no longer recommended by Google
Terraform replaces the need for IAM and inherently enforces least-privilege by design
Terraform stores desired state in a state file (often in Cloud Storage with state locking) and reconciles on plan/apply runs
Config Connector continuously reconciles Google Cloud resources from Kubernetes custom resources using a controller
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Config Connector is an actively supported, recommended Google product, not the legacy successor to Deployment Manager. Option B is wrong because Terraform is an IaC tool; IAM is a separate Google Cloud service that Terraform configures, not replaces. Option C is correct because Terraform's model is a state file (typically a Cloud Storage backend with object versioning and state locking) plus plan/apply runs that reconcile state at execution time. Option D is correct because Config Connector installs a controller in the cluster that continuously reconciles Google Cloud resources from Kubernetes custom resources, just like any operator pattern.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Config Connector is an actively supported, recommended Google product, not the legacy successor to Deployment Manager.
Option B is wrong because Terraform is an IaC tool; IAM is a separate Google Cloud service that Terraform configures, not replaces.
Option C is correct because Terraform's model is a state file (typically a Cloud Storage backend with object versioning and state locking) plus plan/apply runs that reconcile state at execution time.
Option D is correct because Config Connector installs a controller in the cluster that continuously reconciles Google Cloud resources from Kubernetes custom resources, just like any operator pattern.

questions.question: An engineering manager asks how Gemini Cloud Assist can accelerate writing Terraform for a multi-region web app. Which best describes how Gemini Cloud Assist supports IaC authoring?
options: It rewrites all existing Terraform in the repository overnight to match Google's latest published module conventions, committing changes to the main branch automatically
It generates Terraform snippets and explains resource configs in the Cloud Console for engineers to review
It deploys Terraform modules directly into production accounts without requiring any change-management or CI/CD pipeline approval steps
It is exclusively a code-completion plugin for VS Code and is therefore not available inside the Cloud Console for engineers working in a browser
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Gemini Cloud Assist suggests changes; it does not perform large-scale unattended rewrites of a repository. Option B is correct because Gemini Cloud Assist provides AI-assisted IaC and gcloud snippet generation, plus explanations of resource configuration, directly inside the Cloud Console; engineers review the suggested Terraform and commit it through their normal change process. Option C is wrong because Gemini Cloud Assist does not bypass CI/CD; it accelerates authoring, not deployment. Option D is wrong because Gemini Cloud Assist is integrated into the Cloud Console (and Gemini Code Assist is the IDE-side counterpart); the assistant in question is not exclusive to VS Code.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Gemini Cloud Assist suggests changes; it does not perform large-scale unattended rewrites of a repository.

Option B is correct because Gemini Cloud Assist provides AI-assisted IaC and gcloud snippet generation, plus explanations of resource configuration, directly inside the Cloud Console; engineers review the suggested Terraform and commit it through their normal change process.
Option C is wrong because Gemini Cloud Assist does not bypass CI/CD; it accelerates authoring, not deployment.
Option D is wrong because Gemini Cloud Assist is integrated into the Cloud Console (and Gemini Code Assist is the IDE-side counterpart); the assistant in question is not exclusive to VS Code.

questions.question: A retail company replicating its OLTP MySQL database into BigQuery for near-real-time analytics needs a managed change-data-capture pipeline that reads MySQL binlog events and writes them to BigQuery. Which Google Cloud product should they use?
options: Database Migration Service configured to migrate MySQL into BigQuery directly
Storage Transfer Service to copy nightly mysqldump files to Cloud Storage and load into BigQuery
Datastream with a MySQL source connector and a BigQuery destination
Pub/Sub to BigQuery subscriptions, with the application writing every database change to a topic
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Database Migration Service is for migrating databases between like engines (MySQL to MySQL on Cloud SQL, for example) with minimal downtime; it does not target BigQuery. Option B is wrong because nightly dumps are batch, not near-real-time, and contradict the CDC requirement. Option C is correct because Datastream is Google's managed serverless CDC service: it captures change events from MySQL (and PostgreSQL/Oracle) and writes them to destinations including BigQuery for analytics. Option D is wrong because forcing the application to dual-write to Pub/Sub adds invasive code, risks consistency drift between the database and the topic, and is not the managed CDC approach the team wants.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Database Migration Service is for migrating databases between like engines (MySQL to MySQL on Cloud SQL, for example) with minimal downtime; it does not target BigQuery.
Option B is wrong because nightly dumps are batch, not near-real-time, and contradict the CDC requirement.

Option C is correct because Datastream is Google's managed serverless CDC service: it captures change events from MySQL (and PostgreSQL/Oracle) and writes them to destinations including BigQuery for analytics.

Option D is wrong because forcing the application to dual-write to Pub/Sub adds invasive code, risks consistency drift between the database and the topic, and is not the managed CDC approach the team wants.

questions.question: A team running Spanner workloads wants to write integration tests in CI that exercise real query plans and transactions without consuming a production Spanner instance. They want fast startup and no billing charges. Which approach should you recommend?
options: Spin up a small Spanner instance in a dedicated test project per CI job and tear it down afterwards
Mock the Spanner client library in test code and only run real Spanner queries during the nightly E2E suite
Replicate Spanner queries to a Postgres instance in CI and run tests there, relying on syntactic similarity
Run the Cloud Spanner emulator (gcloud emulators spanner) in CI and point SPANNER_EMULATOR_HOST at it
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because spinning up real Spanner instances per CI job is slow, expensive, and unnecessary when a free emulator is available. Option B is wrong because mocks do not exercise real Spanner query parsing, transaction semantics, or interleaved tables; bugs found nightly are bugs found late. Option C is wrong because Postgres and Spanner SQL diverge in important ways (especially transactions, interleaving, and STRUCT support), so test results would not be representative. Option D is correct because the Cloud Spanner emulator runs locally (or in a CI container), supports nearly all Spanner SQL and transaction behavior, starts in seconds, and clients connect by setting SPANNER_EMULATOR_HOST to the emulator address.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because spinning up real Spanner instances per CI job is slow, expensive, and unnecessary when a free emulator is available.
Option B is wrong because mocks do not exercise real Spanner query parsing, transaction semantics, or interleaved tables; bugs found nightly are bugs found late.
Option C is wrong because Postgres and Spanner SQL diverge in important ways (especially transactions, interleaving, and STRUCT support), so test results would not be representative.

Option D is correct because the Cloud Spanner emulator runs locally (or in a CI container), supports nearly all Spanner SQL and transaction behavior, starts in seconds, and clients connect by setting SPANNER_EMULATOR_HOST to the emulator address.

questions.question: A platform team is designing a deployment pipeline for a GKE-hosted payment service where any bad rollout must be reversible in seconds. The service handles 5,000 requests per second and has strict latency requirements. Which deployment pattern best fits?
options: Recreate (kubectl set image with maxUnavailable=100%) so the new version replaces the old immediately, accepting brief downtime during the swap
Rolling update with maxSurge=25% and maxUnavailable=25% applied across multiple Deployment generations to gradually roll over the payment service
Blue/green via two Kubernetes Services and a Gateway, switching the active backend by selector after smoke tests
A custom in-pod feature-flag toggle that re-wires traffic to a new code path at runtime, controlled by an external config service and a dashboard
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Recreate causes downtime as the old version is terminated before the new is healthy, which is incompatible with a 5,000 RPS payment service. Option B is wrong because a rolling update interleaves old and new versions and rolling back from a partially rolled-out state still takes minutes; blue/green rolls back in seconds via a single selector flip. Option C is correct because blue/green with two Services and a Gateway/Ingress allows a smoke test against the new (green) deployment in isolation, then a single selector or routing flip switches traffic. Rollback is the inverse flip, completing in seconds. Option D is wrong because in-pod feature flags help with progressive feature exposure but do not replace deployment topology; a bad binary or library change cannot be flagged off.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Recreate causes downtime as the old version is terminated before the new is healthy, which is incompatible with a 5,000 RPS payment service.

Option B is wrong because a rolling update interleaves old and new versions and rolling back from a partially rolled-out state still takes minutes; blue/green rolls back in seconds via a single selector flip.

Option C is correct because blue/green with two Services and a Gateway/Ingress allows a smoke test against the new (green) deployment in isolation, then a single selector or routing flip switches traffic. Rollback is the inverse flip, completing in seconds.
Option D is wrong because in-pod feature flags help with progressive feature exposure but do not replace deployment topology; a bad binary or library change cannot be flagged off.

questions.question: A team is rolling out a new recommendation algorithm to a Cloud Run service. They want to send 1% of production traffic to the new revision, watch error rate and latency for 30 minutes, then ramp to 10%, 50%, and 100% if metrics stay green. Which capability supports this with minimal custom plumbing?
options: Deploy as a brand-new Cloud Run service in a separate project and reverse-proxy 1% of traffic via a custom Nginx
Deploy as a Cloud Functions endpoint and use a Cloud Load Balancing URL map to split traffic between Cloud Run and Cloud Functions
Use Cloud Run revisions with traffic splitting (gcloud run services update-traffic --to-revisions OLD=99,NEW=1) and progressively shift
Run both revisions side by side and write a Pub/Sub fan-out so consumers self-select which revision to call
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because spinning up a separate project and reverse proxy adds significant operational overhead and bypasses Cloud Run's native splitting. Option B is wrong because mixing Cloud Run and Cloud Functions for the canary is unnecessary heterogeneity and does not solve traffic splitting on the same service. Option C is correct because Cloud Run natively supports tagged revisions and weighted traffic splits; the team can run gcloud run services update-traffic with weighted percentages to perform a canary at 1/10/50/100 with no custom plumbing. Option D is wrong because Pub/Sub fan-out is an event pattern, not a request-traffic splitting mechanism, and consumer self-selection is not a controlled canary.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because spinning up a separate project and reverse proxy adds significant operational overhead and bypasses Cloud Run's native splitting.
Option B is wrong because mixing Cloud Run and Cloud Functions for the canary is unnecessary heterogeneity and does not solve traffic splitting on the same service.

Option C is correct because Cloud Run natively supports tagged revisions and weighted traffic splits; the team can run gcloud run services update-traffic with weighted percentages to perform a canary at 1/10/50/100 with no custom plumbing.
Option D is wrong because Pub/Sub fan-out is an event pattern, not a request-traffic splitting mechanism, and consumer self-selection is not a controlled canary.

questions.question: A regulated organization requires that all Terraform changes pass a security policy review before being applied to production projects, with policy expressed as code and evaluated automatically. Which combination correctly delivers this? (Choose two.)
options: Grant the CI service account roles/owner on production projects to remove approval bottlenecks
Use Sentinel or Open Policy Agent (OPA) policies evaluated against the Terraform plan in CI before apply
Disable the Terraform plan phase and run terraform apply directly to reduce drift
Enable Organization Policy constraints (for example, restrict public IPs, enforce CMEK) at the Google Cloud organization level
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because granting roles/owner removes the very controls the requirement asks for and violates least privilege. Option B is correct because Sentinel (Terraform Cloud/Enterprise) and OPA (with conftest or similar) evaluate the Terraform plan against codified policies in CI, blocking risky changes before apply. Option C is wrong because skipping plan removes the artifact policy engines evaluate and increases the chance of unreviewed changes. Option D is correct because Organization Policy constraints enforce guardrails at the platform level (for example, restrict allowed locations, require CMEK, restrict public IP) regardless of how the request was made, complementing the CI-time policy check.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because granting roles/owner removes the very controls the requirement asks for and violates least privilege.
Option B is correct because Sentinel (Terraform Cloud/Enterprise) and OPA (with conftest or similar) evaluate the Terraform plan against codified policies in CI, blocking risky changes before apply.
Option C is wrong because skipping plan removes the artifact policy engines evaluate and increases the chance of unreviewed changes.

Option D is correct because Organization Policy constraints enforce guardrails at the platform level (for example, restrict allowed locations, require CMEK, restrict public IP) regardless of how the request was made, complementing the CI-time policy check.

questions.question: An organization is migrating a 600 TB on-premises NAS archive to Cloud Storage. The site has a 100 Mbps link that is saturated by business traffic, and the network team will not increase bandwidth. The data must arrive within 30 days. Which approach should you recommend?
options: Storage Transfer Service with an on-prem agent, scheduled outside business hours on the existing link
Compress the archive and use gsutil cp -m from a high-CPU VM at the source to maximize throughput
Order one or more Transfer Appliances, copy data locally at LAN speeds, and ship them to Google for ingest
Use Datastream to incrementally pick up file changes and replicate to Cloud Storage over the existing link
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because 600 TB over 100 Mbps off-peak (call it 50 Mbps average) is more than 1,100 days at perfect efficiency; the 30-day requirement cannot be met online. Option B is wrong because compression and parallel gsutil do not change the fundamental bandwidth constraint of a saturated 100 Mbps link. Option C is correct because Transfer Appliance is purpose-built for cases where the online link is the bottleneck; data is copied to the appliance at LAN speed and ingested at Google within the required window. Option D is wrong because Datastream is for database CDC, not bulk NAS archive transfer.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because 600 TB over 100 Mbps off-peak (call it 50 Mbps average) is more than 1,100 days at perfect efficiency; the 30-day requirement cannot be met online.
Option B is wrong because compression and parallel gsutil do not change the fundamental bandwidth constraint of a saturated 100 Mbps link.

Option C is correct because Transfer Appliance is purpose-built for cases where the online link is the bottleneck; data is copied to the appliance at LAN speed and ingested at Google within the required window.
Option D is wrong because Datastream is for database CDC, not bulk NAS archive transfer.

questions.question: An Apigee X proxy must authenticate partner requests by validating a JSON Web Token signed by the partner's IdP. The proxy then must look up an internal customer ID from the token's subject claim and add it as an X-Customer-Id header to the backend request. Which combination of policies should you apply? (Choose two.)
options: VerifyJWT policy referencing a JWKS URI to validate the partner-signed JWT
ServiceCallout to the partner's IdP on every request to ask whether the JWT is valid
AssignMessage policy to set the X-Customer-Id header on the backend request
DecodeJWT only, treating the absence of an exception as proof of authenticity
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because VerifyJWT validates a JWT's signature against a configured JWKS URI (typically a well-known partner endpoint) and checks claims such as iss, aud, and exp, which is exactly the right way to authenticate the partner's JWT. Option B is wrong because hitting the IdP on every request adds latency and creates an availability dependency; signature verification with a cached JWKS does not require a per-request callout. Option C is correct because AssignMessage is the standard policy for setting or modifying headers on a request (or response) flow; the customer ID is set as X-Customer-Id before the backend call. Option D is wrong because DecodeJWT only parses the token; it does not verify the signature, so an attacker could craft tokens that DecodeJWT accepts.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because VerifyJWT validates a JWT's signature against a configured JWKS URI (typically a well-known partner endpoint) and checks claims such as iss, aud, and exp, which is exactly the right way to authenticate the partner's JWT.
Option B is wrong because hitting the IdP on every request adds latency and creates an availability dependency; signature verification with a cached JWKS does not require a per-request callout.
Option C is correct because AssignMessage is the standard policy for setting or modifying headers on a request (or response) flow; the customer ID is set as X-Customer-Id before the backend call.
Option D is wrong because DecodeJWT only parses the token; it does not verify the signature, so an attacker could craft tokens that DecodeJWT accepts.

questions.question: A team uses Terraform for Google Cloud and writes a module that creates a Cloud SQL instance and grants IAM bindings on it. After running terraform apply, a developer modifies the instance in the Cloud Console (changes the machine tier). On the next terraform apply, what should the team expect to happen by default, and how should they handle this kind of drift going forward?
options: Terraform silently adopts the new tier as desired state, recording the console change as the new baseline and proceeding without any diff or warning to the operator
On the next plan, Terraform reverts the tier to the code value; the team should forbid console edits or run terraform import/code update when intentional
Terraform deletes and recreates the Cloud SQL instance to align state, which destroys the data; the team must then restore from a backup before continuing
Terraform refuses to run any further commands until the developer manually edits the state file by hand to match the change made through the console
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Terraform does not silently adopt out-of-band changes; its model is that the .tf files are the source of truth. Option B is correct because terraform refresh (run automatically during plan unless disabled) reads real-world state and produces a plan that reverts the unexpected change back to what the code declares. The right operational practice is to treat console edits as forbidden in IaC-managed projects, or to update code (and run terraform import for new resources) when a change is intentional. Option C is wrong because changing a Cloud SQL machine tier is an in-place update, not a destroy-and-recreate. Option D is wrong because Terraform does not require manual state-file editing; that is an unsafe operation and is not part of the default drift workflow.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Terraform does not silently adopt out-of-band changes; its model is that the .tf files are the source of truth.

Option B is correct because terraform refresh (run automatically during plan unless disabled) reads real-world state and produces a plan that reverts the unexpected change back to what the code declares. The right operational practice is to treat console edits as forbidden in IaC-managed projects, or to update code (and run terraform import for new resources) when a change is intentional.
Option C is wrong because changing a Cloud SQL machine tier is an in-place update, not a destroy-and-recreate.
Option D is wrong because Terraform does not require manual state-file editing; that is an unsafe operation and is not part of the default drift workflow.

questions.question: A FinTech team is migrating an Oracle 19c database with 2 TB of data and complex PL/SQL packages to Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL. They need to convert schema and code, validate behavior, and minimize cutover downtime. Which combination of tools should you recommend? (Choose two.)
options: Datastream to replicate Oracle changes to BigQuery and then export the BigQuery dataset back into Cloud SQL on a nightly schedule via a Dataflow pipeline
Database Migration Service for heterogeneous Oracle-to-PostgreSQL continuous replication
Storage Transfer Service to move the Oracle datafiles to Cloud Storage, then directly attach those raw datafiles into the new Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL instance
An automated schema-conversion tool (Database Migration Assessment or Ora2Pg) to convert schema and PL/SQL before data load
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the destination is Cloud SQL, not BigQuery; using BigQuery as an intermediate stop is unnecessary architectural complexity. Option B is correct because Database Migration Service supports Oracle-to-Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL with continuous replication, enabling minimal-downtime cutover. Option C is wrong because Cloud SQL does not accept attaching raw Oracle datafiles; engines differ fundamentally and require logical migration. Option D is correct because Oracle PL/SQL does not run unchanged on PostgreSQL; a schema-conversion tool (Database Migration Assessment, Ora2Pg, or similar) converts schemas, stored procedures, and packages before data load, and gives a report on items needing manual remediation.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because the destination is Cloud SQL, not BigQuery; using BigQuery as an intermediate stop is unnecessary architectural complexity.
Option B is correct because Database Migration Service supports Oracle-to-Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL with continuous replication, enabling minimal-downtime cutover.
Option C is wrong because Cloud SQL does not accept attaching raw Oracle datafiles; engines differ fundamentally and require logical migration.

Option D is correct because Oracle PL/SQL does not run unchanged on PostgreSQL; a schema-conversion tool (Database Migration Assessment, Ora2Pg, or similar) converts schemas, stored procedures, and packages before data load, and gives a report on items needing manual remediation.

questions.question: An SRE team wants to use Gemini Cloud Assist responsibly when triaging production incidents. The team must avoid making unreviewed changes and must keep humans accountable for decisions. Which single practice best embodies this principle?
options: Configure Gemini Cloud Assist to apply remediation gcloud commands automatically whenever an alert fires in production, so that MTTR drops without any human involvement
Use Gemini Cloud Assist to summarize logs and propose hypotheses that engineers investigate and validate before acting
Rely on Gemini Cloud Assist outputs as legally binding evidence in customer-facing post-incident reports, without further engineer authorship or review
Use Gemini Cloud Assist as the sole source of truth for IAM least-privilege decisions in production projects, bypassing security review and policy documentation
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Gemini Cloud Assist is not designed to silently apply changes in production; doing so removes the human-in-the-loop control that the team explicitly wants. Option B is correct because summarizing logs and proposing hypotheses for engineers to investigate is exactly the kind of low-risk, high-leverage assistance Gemini Cloud Assist is designed to provide; engineers retain decision authority. Option C is wrong because AI-generated text is not, on its own, suitable as binding evidence; engineer-authored post-incident reports remain the source of truth. Option D is wrong because IAM and least-privilege decisions must be grounded in policy and security review; the assistant can help draft but is not authoritative.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Gemini Cloud Assist is not designed to silently apply changes in production; doing so removes the human-in-the-loop control that the team explicitly wants.

Option B is correct because summarizing logs and proposing hypotheses for engineers to investigate is exactly the kind of low-risk, high-leverage assistance Gemini Cloud Assist is designed to provide; engineers retain decision authority.
Option C is wrong because AI-generated text is not, on its own, suitable as binding evidence; engineer-authored post-incident reports remain the source of truth.
Option D is wrong because IAM and least-privilege decisions must be grounded in policy and security review; the assistant can help draft but is not authoritative.

questions.question: A team operates a large Java microservice that needs to handle Google Cloud API errors gracefully. They invoke Cloud Storage and Pub/Sub APIs with the official Google client libraries. Which client-side configuration most directly improves resilience without re-implementing retry logic?
options: Tune the client library's built-in RetrySettings and rely on its idempotency-aware default retry behavior
Write a wrapper around every Google client call that catches all exceptions and retries them in a tight while loop, treating every error as transient
Disable the client library's built-in retries and centralize all retry logic inside a homegrown circuit-breaker library shared across every service in the platform
Switch to direct REST calls using a generic HTTP client so the team explicitly controls every aspect of retries, telemetry, authentication, and pagination by hand
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the Google Cloud client libraries ship with built-in RetrySettings that already implement exponential backoff with jitter, configurable initial/max delays, max attempts, and a list of retriable status codes; they also know which methods are idempotent and safe to retry. Tuning RetrySettings is the direct, supported path to better resilience. Option B is wrong because a tight while loop with broad catch retries non-retriable errors (such as 400 Bad Request), causing wasted calls and masking bugs. Option C is wrong because disabling the library's retries discards well-tested behavior; a custom circuit breaker can complement retries but should not replace the library's per-method idempotency awareness. Option D is wrong because dropping the official client library forces the team to re-implement auth refresh, retries, telemetry, and pagination, which is the opposite of what the requirement asks.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because the Google Cloud client libraries ship with built-in RetrySettings that already implement exponential backoff with jitter, configurable initial/max delays, max attempts, and a list of retriable status codes; they also know which methods are idempotent and safe to retry. Tuning RetrySettings is the direct, supported path to better resilience.
Option B is wrong because a tight while loop with broad catch retries non-retriable errors (such as 400 Bad Request), causing wasted calls and masking bugs.

Option C is wrong because disabling the library's retries discards well-tested behavior; a custom circuit breaker can complement retries but should not replace the library's per-method idempotency awareness.
Option D is wrong because dropping the official client library forces the team to re-implement auth refresh, retries, telemetry, and pagination, which is the opposite of what the requirement asks.

questions.question: A platform team is adopting the operational excellence pillar of the Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework for a customer-facing API. Which combination of practices most directly reflects the pillar's core principles?
options: Manually approving every production change in a weekly change-advisory board meeting and writing incident summaries only when customers escalate complaints to the support desk for tracking

Defining documented operational processes, automating routine toil with Terraform and Cloud Build, designing for observability with structured logs and metrics, and running blameless postmortems after every incident
Sizing Compute Engine instances 2x above projected peak load to eliminate the need for autoscaling, paging, runbooks, or any post-incident review cycle on production services
Standardizing on a single project for all environments so that all logs, metrics, and IAM bindings are co-located, simplifying day-to-day operations for the on-call rotation team
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because manual approvals and ad-hoc summaries contradict the principles of automation and learning from every incident, not just escalated ones. Option B is correct because it pairs all four operational excellence principles: defined processes, automation of toil, observability by design, and blameless learning from incidents. Option C is wrong because over-provisioning hardware does not replace the need for observability, runbooks, and incident review, and conflicts with cost-efficiency goals. Option D is wrong because a single project for all environments removes blast-radius isolation and complicates IAM, violating the framework's separation guidance.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because manual approvals and ad-hoc summaries contradict the principles of automation and learning from every incident, not just escalated ones.
Option B is correct because it pairs all four operational excellence principles: defined processes, automation of toil, observability by design, and blameless learning from incidents.
Option C is wrong because over-provisioning hardware does not replace the need for observability, runbooks, and incident review, and conflicts with cost-efficiency goals.
Option D is wrong because a single project for all environments removes blast-radius isolation and complicates IAM, violating the framework's separation guidance.

questions.question: Your SRE team wants ops dashboards that align with the Well-Architected Framework recommendation for shared operational responsibility. Which dashboard design choice best supports that recommendation?
options: Build one combined dashboard per service that exposes SLI compliance, error budget burn, latency percentiles, and saturation, and grant view access to both developers and operators of that service
Restrict all production dashboards to the central SRE team only so that developer teams cannot accidentally misinterpret signals while triaging issues during business hours
Build a single org-wide dashboard listing every service's raw CPU and memory metrics so leadership can scan utilization without needing per-service context or service-specific signals
Replace dashboards with weekly PDF reports emailed to product owners summarizing the previous week's incidents and uptime, eliminating the need for real-time monitoring screens
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because shared responsibility requires both developers and operators to see the same signals (SLI, error budget, latency, saturation) for the services they own. Option B is wrong because hiding dashboards from developers undermines the shared-responsibility principle and slows incident triage. Option C is wrong because raw CPU/memory without service context is a saturation-only view and obscures user-facing reliability signals. Option D is wrong because weekly PDFs are not real-time and cannot support the operational excellence principle of designing for observability.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because shared responsibility requires both developers and operators to see the same signals (SLI, error budget, latency, saturation) for the services they own.
Option B is wrong because hiding dashboards from developers undermines the shared-responsibility principle and slows incident triage.
Option C is wrong because raw CPU/memory without service context is a saturation-only view and obscures user-facing reliability signals.
Option D is wrong because weekly PDFs are not real-time and cannot support the operational excellence principle of designing for observability.

questions.question: A team is debating whether to write runbooks for routine production tasks. Which justification most directly reflects the Well-Architected operational excellence guidance?
options: Runbooks codify the steps for repeatable operations and incident response, reduce reliance on tribal knowledge during on-call, and create an artifact that can be reviewed, tested, and ultimately automated
Runbooks are mainly an audit artifact required by regulators in financial services and are unnecessary for engineering teams whose services are deployed on managed Google Cloud products
Runbooks should be written only by managers because operational engineers tend to skip them at 3 a.m. and prefer to debug from first principles regardless of the documented procedure
Runbooks can replace alerting policies and SLOs because a well-written runbook tells the on-call engineer exactly when to take action without needing a separate notification channel
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because runbooks support repeatability, reduce tribal knowledge, and serve as the input for automation, which are core operational excellence principles. Option B is wrong because runbooks are an engineering best practice regardless of regulation or whether services run on managed products. Option C is wrong because authorship belongs to the engineers who know the procedures; manager-only authorship is not a real-world pattern. Option D is wrong because runbooks do not replace alerting or SLOs; they complement them by guiding response when alerts fire.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because runbooks support repeatability, reduce tribal knowledge, and serve as the input for automation, which are core operational excellence principles.
Option B is wrong because runbooks are an engineering best practice regardless of regulation or whether services run on managed products.
Option C is wrong because authorship belongs to the engineers who know the procedures; manager-only authorship is not a real-world pattern.
Option D is wrong because runbooks do not replace alerting or SLOs; they complement them by guiding response when alerts fire.

questions.question: Your application emits per-request structured JSON logs to Cloud Logging. The data engineering team needs to run ad-hoc SQL across 90 days of logs for trend analysis. Which sink configuration best meets the requirement while controlling cost?
options: Create a Cloud Logging sink that exports the relevant log filter to a Pub/Sub topic, then have a Dataflow job stream the messages into a Cloud SQL instance with a 90-day retention policy
Create a Cloud Logging sink that exports the relevant log filter to a partitioned BigQuery table and rely on BigQuery's table expiration to manage retention while enabling ad-hoc SQL queries
Increase the retention of the _Default log bucket to 90 days and query the logs directly using Logs Explorer's advanced filters, which provide a SQL-compatible interface to logs in place
Create a Cloud Logging sink that exports the relevant log filter to a Cloud Storage bucket in Nearline storage class so the data engineering team can mount it to BigQuery via external tables
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Cloud SQL is not optimized for ad-hoc analytical SQL across high-volume log data and adds a Dataflow streaming hop that is unnecessary. Option B is correct because a BigQuery sink with partitioned tables and table expiration is the recommended pattern for ad-hoc SQL over logs while controlling cost via partition pruning and TTL. Option C is wrong because Logs Explorer is not SQL-compatible and extending _Default bucket retention does not enable analytical SQL. Option D is wrong because GCS Nearline external tables work for archival but are slower and more expensive for repeated ad-hoc analytical queries than native BigQuery storage.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Cloud SQL is not optimized for ad-hoc analytical SQL across high-volume log data and adds a Dataflow streaming hop that is unnecessary.
Option B is correct because a BigQuery sink with partitioned tables and table expiration is the recommended pattern for ad-hoc SQL over logs while controlling cost via partition pruning and TTL.
Option C is wrong because Logs Explorer is not SQL-compatible and extending _Default bucket retention does not enable analytical SQL.
Option D is wrong because GCS Nearline external tables work for archival but are slower and more expensive for repeated ad-hoc analytical queries than native BigQuery storage.

questions.question: You are designing a logging strategy for a regulated workload. Which combination of Cloud Logging features and controls is appropriate? (Choose 2.)
options: Route Data Access audit logs to a dedicated log bucket with a custom retention period and apply a locked retention policy to prevent early deletion
Configure the _Required log bucket retention to 30 days to reduce storage cost for admin activity audit logs across the entire organization
Create an aggregated organization-level sink that exports admin activity logs to a Cloud Storage bucket with Bucket Lock and an object retention policy for tamper-evident archival
Disable the _Default log bucket to stop ingestion of application logs and rely solely on each service's stdout output for debugging during production incidents
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Data Access logs in a dedicated bucket with a locked retention policy give configurable retention with immutability guarantees suitable for a regulated workload. Option B is wrong because the _Required bucket retention is fixed at 400 days by Google Cloud and cannot be shortened; admin activity audit logs are retained there regardless of cost preference. Option C is correct because an aggregated org-level sink to a Cloud Storage bucket with Bucket Lock provides tamper-evident long-term archival of admin activity logs across the organization. Option D is wrong because disabling _Default loses logs and stdout alone is not a compliant logging strategy.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because Data Access logs in a dedicated bucket with a locked retention policy give configurable retention with immutability guarantees suitable for a regulated workload.
Option B is wrong because the _Required bucket retention is fixed at 400 days by Google Cloud and cannot be shortened; admin activity audit logs are retained there regardless of cost preference.
Option C is correct because an aggregated org-level sink to a Cloud Storage bucket with Bucket Lock provides tamper-evident long-term archival of admin activity logs across the organization.
Option D is wrong because disabling _Default loses logs and stdout alone is not a compliant logging strategy.

questions.question: You need a metric that counts how many times the string 'PaymentDeclined' appears in your application's logs so you can alert when the rate exceeds a threshold. Which Cloud Logging feature most directly supports this requirement?
options: Create a Cloud Trace span filter that captures the payment service spans and emit a metric derived from the span attribute carrying the decline reason for use in alerting policies
Create a counter log-based metric in Cloud Logging with a filter that matches the 'PaymentDeclined' string, then build a Cloud Monitoring alert policy over that metric
Create a synthetic monitor that runs a test transaction every minute and emits a custom metric whenever the response body contains the 'PaymentDeclined' marker for downstream alerting
Enable Cloud Profiler on the payment service and configure it to alert when wall-clock time spent in the decline handler exceeds a configurable percentage of total CPU time
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Cloud Trace measures latency of spans; it is not a substring counter over logs and is not designed to drive simple count-based alerts. Option B is correct because counter log-based metrics are the purpose-built feature for turning log line matches into time-series metrics that Cloud Monitoring alert policies can evaluate. Option C is wrong because synthetic monitors test a single probe and would not count real-traffic decline events; that pattern misses production volume. Option D is wrong because Cloud Profiler measures code performance, not log line occurrences; it cannot count business events.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Cloud Trace measures latency of spans; it is not a substring counter over logs and is not designed to drive simple count-based alerts.
Option B is correct because counter log-based metrics are the purpose-built feature for turning log line matches into time-series metrics that Cloud Monitoring alert policies can evaluate.
Option C is wrong because synthetic monitors test a single probe and would not count real-traffic decline events; that pattern misses production volume.
Option D is wrong because Cloud Profiler measures code performance, not log line occurrences; it cannot count business events.

questions.question: A Cloud Run service shows steady-state CPU at 45% but its p99 latency has doubled. Cloud Profiler is enabled. Which step is most likely to identify the cause without changing code first?
options: Switch the profiler view to heap profiling and look for the largest allocations, since heap pressure is the most common cause of latency regressions in containerized services on Cloud Run
Increase the CPU allocation of the Cloud Run revision to give the service more headroom; the doubled latency suggests CPU saturation despite the moderate steady-state utilization figure
Switch to contention profiling and tune the database connection pool size, since p99 doubling in a Cloud Run service is almost always caused by lock contention on connection pool acquisition
Open Cloud Profiler's wall-clock view filtered by service_version of the slow revision and inspect functions that appear hot in wall-clock but cold in CPU time to find off-CPU waits
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because heap profiling helps with memory pressure or GC pauses, not directly with latency unless GC is implicated; that is a guess, not a diagnostic step. Option B is wrong because adding CPU without a root cause is a workaround and the data (45% steady CPU) does not point to CPU saturation. Option C is wrong because contention profiling is a candidate but assuming connection pool contention without evidence is not a diagnostic step. Option D is correct because wall-clock profiling reveals functions blocked on I/O or RPCs that look cold in CPU time but dominate latency; filtering by service_version isolates the affected revision.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because heap profiling helps with memory pressure or GC pauses, not directly with latency unless GC is implicated; that is a guess, not a diagnostic step.
Option B is wrong because adding CPU without a root cause is a workaround and the data (45% steady CPU) does not point to CPU saturation.
Option C is wrong because contention profiling is a candidate but assuming connection pool contention without evidence is not a diagnostic step.
Option D is correct because wall-clock profiling reveals functions blocked on I/O or RPCs that look cold in CPU time but dominate latency; filtering by service_version isolates the affected revision.

questions.question: Your team uses OpenTelemetry to instrument a polyglot microservices fleet on GKE and ships traces to Cloud Trace. Which propagation header should you standardize on for trace context across services?
options: x-cloud-trace-context as the canonical header because it is the Google-native format and Cloud Trace exclusively understands this legacy GCP format from older Stackdriver client libraries
traceparent as defined by the W3C Trace Context specification, which is the OpenTelemetry default and is supported end-to-end by Cloud Trace ingestion
x-b3-traceid because Zipkin's B3 multi-header format is the industry default for OpenTelemetry SDKs and is required for ingestion into Cloud Trace from non-Google services
x-amzn-trace-id rebadged in a custom propagator because it provides the richest segment metadata across cloud providers and integrates natively with Cloud Trace via the AWS SDK shim
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because x-cloud-trace-context is supported but is not the OpenTelemetry default; the modern recommendation is W3C traceparent. Option B is correct because W3C Trace Context (traceparent) is the OpenTelemetry default propagator and is ingested by Cloud Trace, making it the right standardization for a polyglot fleet. Option C is wrong because B3 is one of several supported formats but is not the OpenTelemetry SDK default. Option D is wrong because x-amzn-trace-id is AWS X-Ray's format and is not appropriate for a GKE + Cloud Trace deployment.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because x-cloud-trace-context is supported but is not the OpenTelemetry default; the modern recommendation is W3C traceparent.
Option B is correct because W3C Trace Context (traceparent) is the OpenTelemetry default propagator and is ingested by Cloud Trace, making it the right standardization for a polyglot fleet.
Option C is wrong because B3 is one of several supported formats but is not the OpenTelemetry SDK default.
Option D is wrong because x-amzn-trace-id is AWS X-Ray's format and is not appropriate for a GKE + Cloud Trace deployment.

questions.question: A service exposes a /metrics endpoint in Prometheus exposition format on GKE. You want this scraped into Cloud Monitoring without running your own Prometheus server. Which approach is recommended?
options: Deploy a Cloud Monitoring agent on each Compute Engine VM in the cluster and point it at the /metrics endpoint URL via static configuration files maintained by the platform team
Use Cloud Monitoring's legacy custom metric ingestion API and write a sidecar that polls /metrics every 60 seconds, parses the Prometheus exposition format, and posts each series to the API
Enable Google Cloud Managed Service for Prometheus in collection mode on the GKE cluster, which scrapes /metrics endpoints and stores series queryable via PromQL in Cloud Monitoring
Export /metrics to Pub/Sub via a Dataflow pipeline that transforms Prometheus exposition to JSON, then ingest the JSON into Cloud Logging and create log-based metrics for each Prometheus series
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the legacy Monitoring agent for VMs is not the scraping path for GKE-hosted Prometheus endpoints and Compute Engine VMs are not the GKE workload runtime. Option B is wrong because writing a sidecar to post each series to the custom metric API is high-toil and ignores the purpose-built managed Prometheus product. Option C is correct because Google Cloud Managed Service for Prometheus provides managed scrape and storage of Prometheus metrics with PromQL queryability, which is the recommended path. Option D is wrong because logs-based metrics from a Pub/Sub pipeline is a roundabout and lossy way to ingest Prometheus data compared to Managed Service for Prometheus.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because the legacy Monitoring agent for VMs is not the scraping path for GKE-hosted Prometheus endpoints and Compute Engine VMs are not the GKE workload runtime.
Option B is wrong because writing a sidecar to post each series to the custom metric API is high-toil and ignores the purpose-built managed Prometheus product.
Option C is correct because Google Cloud Managed Service for Prometheus provides managed scrape and storage of Prometheus metrics with PromQL queryability, which is the recommended path.
Option D is wrong because logs-based metrics from a Pub/Sub pipeline is a roundabout and lossy way to ingest Prometheus data compared to Managed Service for Prometheus.

questions.question: Your team runs an HTTP API behind an external HTTPS load balancer. You want to be alerted when the global endpoint is unreachable from multiple regions for more than 5 minutes. Which feature is most appropriate?
options: Cloud Profiler's wall-clock view set to alert when the request handler's wall-clock latency exceeds a configurable threshold across the regions where the API is deployed and reachable
Cloud Logging counter log-based metric on the load balancer access logs combined with an alert policy that fires when the count of 200 responses drops below an expected lower bound
Cloud Trace span filter targeting the load balancer's frontend span and an alert policy on the trace ingestion rate so a sudden drop indicates the endpoint is unreachable from the public internet
Cloud Monitoring uptime check configured against the public endpoint from multiple geographic regions, paired with an alert policy on the uptime check failure metric over a 5-minute window
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Cloud Profiler measures internal performance, not external reachability; it cannot detect unreachable public endpoints from outside the network. Option B is wrong because dropping 200-response counts can have many causes (rollout, traffic drop) and is a much noisier signal than a purpose-built reachability check. Option C is wrong because Cloud Trace ingestion is not a reachability monitor and depends on traffic existing in the first place. Option D is correct because Cloud Monitoring uptime checks probe from multiple geographic locations and the failure metric is the direct signal an alert policy should observe for reachability.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Cloud Profiler measures internal performance, not external reachability; it cannot detect unreachable public endpoints from outside the network.
Option B is wrong because dropping 200-response counts can have many causes (rollout, traffic drop) and is a much noisier signal than a purpose-built reachability check.
Option C is wrong because Cloud Trace ingestion is not a reachability monitor and depends on traffic existing in the first place.
Option D is correct because Cloud Monitoring uptime checks probe from multiple geographic locations and the failure metric is the direct signal an alert policy should observe for reachability.

questions.question: Your service has a 99.9% availability SLO over a 30-day window. You are designing alerting per the SRE workbook. Which alert configurations together form a multi-window, multi-burn-rate strategy? (Choose 2.)
options: A page-severity alert when the 1-hour burn rate exceeds 14.4 and the 5-minute burn rate also exceeds 14.4, indicating consumption of 2% of the monthly budget within the alerting window
A page-severity alert when the cumulative monthly error rate exceeds 0.1% measured at the end of each calendar month so the alert always corresponds to final budget exhaustion across the window
A ticket-severity alert when the 6-hour burn rate exceeds 6 and the 30-minute burn rate also exceeds 6, indicating a slower budget burn that warrants attention but not immediate paging
A ticket-severity alert when CPU utilization exceeds 80% for 15 minutes, since CPU saturation is the strongest leading indicator of upcoming SLO violations across all service architectures
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because a 14.4x burn over 1 hour consumes 2% of the monthly budget and pairing it with a short 5-minute window reduces false positives, which is the canonical fast-burn page configuration. Option B is wrong because end-of-month evaluation is too late; the alert fires only after the budget is exhausted. Option C is correct because a 6x burn over 6 hours is the canonical slow-burn ticket configuration that catches sustained low-rate budget loss. Option D is wrong because CPU saturation is a resource signal, not an SLI; it should not drive SLO-based pages.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because a 14.4x burn over 1 hour consumes 2% of the monthly budget and pairing it with a short 5-minute window reduces false positives, which is the canonical fast-burn page configuration.
Option B is wrong because end-of-month evaluation is too late; the alert fires only after the budget is exhausted.
Option C is correct because a 6x burn over 6 hours is the canonical slow-burn ticket configuration that catches sustained low-rate budget loss.
Option D is wrong because CPU saturation is a resource signal, not an SLI; it should not drive SLO-based pages.

questions.question: Your stakeholders are confused about SLI, SLO, and SLA. Which definition set is correct?
options: SLI is the indicator (a ratio of good events to valid events), SLO is the target value the SLI should meet over a window, and SLA is an external agreement with consequences if the SLO is missed
SLI is the agreement signed with customers, SLO is the maximum number of incidents allowed per quarter, and SLA is an indicator value computed from raw telemetry over a sliding window
SLI, SLO, and SLA are interchangeable abbreviations used by different teams to describe the same availability percentage measured by uptime checks over a 30-day rolling window
SLI is the alerting threshold in Cloud Monitoring, SLO is a synonym for error budget remaining, and SLA is the latency objective enforced by the load balancer's outlier detection feature
questions.explanation: Option A is correct: an SLI is the indicator (typically good/valid event ratio), an SLO is the target the SLI must meet over a window, and an SLA is an external contract with consequences for missing the SLO. Option B is wrong because it swaps the meanings of SLI and SLA. Option C is wrong because the three terms are distinct levels of abstraction and not interchangeable. Option D is wrong because none of the definitions match the Google SRE definitions; SLI is not an alerting threshold and SLA is not a latency outlier policy.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct: an SLI is the indicator (typically good/valid event ratio), an SLO is the target the SLI must meet over a window, and an SLA is an external contract with consequences for missing the SLO.
Option B is wrong because it swaps the meanings of SLI and SLA.
Option C is wrong because the three terms are distinct levels of abstraction and not interchangeable.
Option D is wrong because none of the definitions match the Google SRE definitions; SLI is not an alerting threshold and SLA is not a latency outlier policy.

questions.question: Your team measures availability as a ratio of successful HTTP requests to total valid requests. You want to exclude requests from synthetic monitors and load tests from the SLI to avoid distorting the measurement. Which approach is best?
options: Tag synthetic and load-test traffic with a distinguishing HTTP header at the source and filter those requests out of the SLI's good and valid event counts in Cloud Monitoring's SLI query
Lower the SLO target by a percentage equal to the expected fraction of synthetic and test traffic so the overall ratio still represents real-user experience without per-request filtering logic
Run synthetic and load-test traffic through a separate Cloud Load Balancer hostname so all of its requests are physically isolated from the production SLI even though it duplicates infrastructure
Discard the request-ratio SLI entirely and rely on Cloud Trace span counts as the primary SLI, since traces from internal probes are automatically excluded from SLO calculations by default
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because filtering by a distinguishing header in the SLI's numerator and denominator is the canonical way to exclude non-user traffic from availability calculations. Option B is wrong because adjusting the SLO target is arbitrary, not principled, and does not actually remove the distortion. Option C is wrong because it requires duplicating infrastructure and is operationally heavy compared to header-based filtering. Option D is wrong because Cloud Trace is not a substitute for an event-ratio SLI and does not automatically exclude any traffic.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because filtering by a distinguishing header in the SLI's numerator and denominator is the canonical way to exclude non-user traffic from availability calculations.
Option B is wrong because adjusting the SLO target is arbitrary, not principled, and does not actually remove the distortion.
Option C is wrong because it requires duplicating infrastructure and is operationally heavy compared to header-based filtering.
Option D is wrong because Cloud Trace is not a substitute for an event-ratio SLI and does not automatically exclude any traffic.

questions.question: You are implementing a canary release for a Cloud Run service using Cloud Deploy. Which configuration most accurately describes a canary rollout?
options: Deploy two equal-traffic revisions in parallel, then use Cloud Deploy's verify step to compare metrics; if both revisions report similar SLI compliance, promote whichever happened to roll out first
Deploy the new revision with zero traffic for 24 hours so it can warm up its caches, then atomically cut 100% of traffic to the new revision once the warm-up timer in the pipeline expires
Deploy the new revision to a separate Cloud Run service in a different region and use Cloud DNS weighted records to route 5% of users to it for a measurable period before flipping all DNS weight
Deploy the new revision and route a small percentage of production traffic to it (e.g., 5%), verify SLI compliance via Cloud Deploy's verify step, then progressively promote traffic to 100%
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because a 50/50 split is not a canary; canary specifically means a small percentage to limit blast radius before promotion. Option B is wrong because zero-traffic warm-up followed by an atomic cutover is closer to blue-green, not canary. Option C is wrong because DNS-based weighting is slow to converge due to client caching and is not how Cloud Deploy implements canary on Cloud Run. Option D is correct because canary deployments send a small percentage of real traffic to the new revision and progressively promote, which Cloud Deploy supports natively with verify and promote steps.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because a 50/50 split is not a canary; canary specifically means a small percentage to limit blast radius before promotion.
Option B is wrong because zero-traffic warm-up followed by an atomic cutover is closer to blue-green, not canary.
Option C is wrong because DNS-based weighting is slow to converge due to client caching and is not how Cloud Deploy implements canary on Cloud Run.

Option D is correct because canary deployments send a small percentage of real traffic to the new revision and progressively promote, which Cloud Deploy supports natively with verify and promote steps.

questions.question: A platform team is choosing between blue-green and rolling deployments for a stateless GKE service. Which difference is correct?
options: Blue-green requires zero downtime during cutover only when the service is stateful; for stateless workloads, rolling deployments inherently produce more downtime than blue-green during any update
Rolling deployments require Anthos Service Mesh for traffic shifting, while blue-green deployments can be performed natively in GKE without service mesh capabilities to support rollback
Blue-green deployments mutate pods in place by patching the container image label, while rolling deployments allocate a separate node pool for the new version and drain the old node pool

Blue-green deployments allocate parallel capacity for both old and new versions during cutover and can instantly roll back by switching traffic; rolling deployments incrementally replace pods and roll back by re-deploying the prior version
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because rolling deployments do not inherently produce more downtime than blue-green for stateless workloads; both can be zero-downtime. Option B is wrong because rolling deployments are a native Kubernetes capability and do not require Anthos Service Mesh. Option C is wrong because the description of blue-green and rolling deployments is inverted and inaccurate; blue-green typically uses parallel deployments, not in-place patching. Option D is correct: blue-green keeps both versions running and switches traffic atomically, enabling instant rollback at the cost of double capacity; rolling deployments incrementally replace pods and roll back by re-deploying the prior version.
