Replace Redis with Firestore in Datastore mode, configured with high write throughput so it behaves like an in-memory cache for the catalog
Replace Redis with Memorystore for Memcached because Memcached delivers higher throughput than Redis for read-heavy catalog workloads
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because self-managing Redis on GKE shifts the operational burden of patching, failover, persistence, and capacity planning onto Cymbal, contradicting the modernization goal of reducing manual effort. Option B is correct because Memorystore for Redis is the Google Cloud managed Redis service: it is wire-compatible with the open-source Redis protocol, so Cymbal's existing Redis client libraries continue to work, and it provides managed HA, failover, and patching. Option C is wrong because Firestore in Datastore mode is a NoSQL document database, not an in-memory cache; latency, cost, and access patterns all differ from Redis. Option D is wrong because Memcached lacks the data structures, persistence, and replication features Cymbal already depends on in Redis, and changing protocols would require client library changes.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because self-managing Redis on GKE shifts the operational burden of patching, failover, persistence, and capacity planning onto Cymbal, contradicting the modernization goal of reducing manual effort.

Option B is correct because Memorystore for Redis is the Google Cloud managed Redis service: it is wire-compatible with the open-source Redis protocol, so Cymbal's existing Redis client libraries continue to work, and it provides managed HA, failover, and patching.
Option C is wrong because Firestore in Datastore mode is a NoSQL document database, not an in-memory cache; latency, cost, and access patterns all differ from Redis.
Option D is wrong because Memcached lacks the data structures, persistence, and replication features Cymbal already depends on in Redis, and changing protocols would require client library changes.
questions.category: Designing and planning a cloud solution architecture

questions.question: Cymbal Retail's executive team has classified the product catalog and order systems as Tier 1: a regional outage must not stop customers from browsing or placing orders. The architecture team must define a business continuity posture for the migrated catalog database. Which TWO actions together best meet this requirement? (Choose two.)
options: Run Cloud SQL for MySQL as a single zonal instance and rely on automated backups to restore service after a regional outage event
Configure the Cloud SQL for MySQL primary as a regional HA instance and add cross-region read replicas that can be promoted on failover
Front the storefront with an external Application Load Balancer using serverless NEGs and Cloud Run services in multiple regions
Store the catalog only in a multi-region Cloud Storage bucket and have the storefront read product data directly from JSON files served from the bucket
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because a single zonal instance fails when its zone fails and backups give an RPO measured in hours, which violates the Tier 1 requirement. Option B is correct because a Cloud SQL HA (regional) configuration survives zone failure within a region and cross-region read replicas can be promoted to recover from a regional failure, giving the catalog database a regional-resilience posture. Option C is correct because pairing the database posture with a multi-region front end via an external Application Load Balancer and serverless NEGs ensures customer traffic can still reach a healthy region during a regional outage. Option D is wrong because static JSON in Cloud Storage cannot satisfy the relational query patterns Cymbal's storefront uses, and it does not address the database tier at all.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because a single zonal instance fails when its zone fails and backups give an RPO measured in hours, which violates the Tier 1 requirement.

Option B is correct because a Cloud SQL HA (regional) configuration survives zone failure within a region and cross-region read replicas can be promoted to recover from a regional failure, giving the catalog database a regional-resilience posture.

Option C is correct because pairing the database posture with a multi-region front end via an external Application Load Balancer and serverless NEGs ensures customer traffic can still reach a healthy region during a regional outage.
Option D is wrong because static JSON in Cloud Storage cannot satisfy the relational query patterns Cymbal's storefront uses, and it does not address the database tier at all.
questions.category: Designing and planning a cloud solution architecture

questions.question: Cymbal Retail wants to automate the catalog and content enrichment pipeline so that supplier feeds are processed into structured product attributes, descriptions, and category-aligned tags. The team needs a managed, repeatable workflow that orchestrates Vertex AI prompts, evaluation steps, and writes the results to the catalog database with full lineage. Which service should they use?
options: Cloud Composer DAGs that shell out to gcloud commands to call Vertex AI endpoints and then run bash scripts to update the catalog rows
Cloud Run jobs triggered by Pub/Sub that each invoke a separate prompt and write results directly to Cloud SQL with no orchestration layer between them
Cloud Workflows steps that call Vertex AI generative-model REST endpoints, leaving retry, evaluation, and lineage tracking to the application code
Vertex AI Pipelines using the KFP SDK to orchestrate prompt steps, evaluation, HITL routing, and catalog writes with built-in lineage
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Cloud Composer with bash-style shell-outs is brittle, lacks ML lineage tracking, and ignores the purpose-built ML orchestration capabilities Vertex AI Pipelines provides. Option B is wrong because Cloud Run jobs with no orchestrator make ordering, retries, and lineage the developer's problem, and writing directly to Cloud SQL bypasses the HITL review the case requires. Option C is wrong because Cloud Workflows can call Vertex AI, but it does not provide the ML-specific evaluation, parameter tracking, or component reuse that an attribute-generation pipeline needs at catalog scale. Option D is correct because Vertex AI Pipelines is the purpose-built managed orchestrator for ML workflows: components, parameters, and artifacts are tracked in Vertex ML Metadata, retries and conditional branching are built in, and HITL approval steps can be modeled as pipeline components before the catalog write.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Cloud Composer with bash-style shell-outs is brittle, lacks ML lineage tracking, and ignores the purpose-built ML orchestration capabilities Vertex AI Pipelines provides.

Option B is wrong because Cloud Run jobs with no orchestrator make ordering, retries, and lineage the developer's problem, and writing directly to Cloud SQL bypasses the HITL review the case requires.

Option C is wrong because Cloud Workflows can call Vertex AI, but it does not provide the ML-specific evaluation, parameter tracking, or component reuse that an attribute-generation pipeline needs at catalog scale.

Option D is correct because Vertex AI Pipelines is the purpose-built managed orchestrator for ML workflows: components, parameters, and artifacts are tracked in Vertex ML Metadata, retries and conditional branching are built in, and HITL approval steps can be modeled as pipeline components before the catalog write.
questions.category: Managing and provisioning a cloud solution infrastructure

questions.question: Cymbal Retail wants to generate alternative product images from a single supplier-provided base image, supporting tasks such as background swaps, color variations, and text overlays for promotional copy. Compliance requires that the model never train on Cymbal's image data and that all generations stay within their Google Cloud project. Which approach satisfies the requirements?
options: Call a third-party public image-generation API from a Cloud Run service that uploads each base image to the provider and stores the result in a Cloud Storage bucket for the storefront
Fine-tune an open-source diffusion model on a GKE GPU node pool using current supplier images as training data, then serve it through a custom Flask endpoint with autoscaling
Use Vertex AI's Generative AI Studio in playground mode by uploading images interactively so associates can generate variations one product at a time through the console
Use Imagen on Vertex AI for image generation and editing through the Vertex AI API, with project-scoped governance and no training on customer data
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because routing supplier images to a third-party API moves Cymbal's catalog data outside their project boundary and conflicts with the requirement that generations stay within their Google Cloud project. Option B is wrong because self-hosting and fine-tuning a diffusion model on GKE adds significant infrastructure, MLOps, and safety-tuning work that Cymbal's modernization goals explicitly try to avoid. Option C is wrong because Generative AI Studio playground is for ad-hoc exploration, not the automated, programmatic image-variation pipeline a catalog of Cymbal's scale needs. Option D is correct because Imagen on Vertex AI provides the required image generation, background editing, and color and text-overlay capabilities through a managed API, and Vertex AI's data governance explicitly states that customer data sent to foundation models is not used to train Google's base models.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because routing supplier images to a third-party API moves Cymbal's catalog data outside their project boundary and conflicts with the requirement that generations stay within their Google Cloud project.

Option B is wrong because self-hosting and fine-tuning a diffusion model on GKE adds significant infrastructure, MLOps, and safety-tuning work that Cymbal's modernization goals explicitly try to avoid.
Option C is wrong because Generative AI Studio playground is for ad-hoc exploration, not the automated, programmatic image-variation pipeline a catalog of Cymbal's scale needs.

Option D is correct because Imagen on Vertex AI provides the required image generation, background editing, and color and text-overlay capabilities through a managed API, and Vertex AI's data governance explicitly states that customer data sent to foundation models is not used to train Google's base models.
questions.category: Managing and provisioning a cloud solution infrastructure

questions.question: Cymbal Retail wants to replace the current category-search experience with natural-language product discovery: a shopper types or speaks a request like 'lightweight waterproof jacket for spring hiking under 100 dollars' and gets ranked, relevant product results. The team prefers a Google Cloud product purpose-built for retail product discovery rather than building a generic search stack. Which service should they adopt?
options: Build a custom semantic search index on top of OpenSearch deployed on a GKE cluster, using embeddings generated from Vertex AI text models for the catalog
Adopt Vertex AI Search for commerce (Discovery AI for retail), ingesting the catalog to serve natural-language queries and ranked product results
Use BigQuery ML's CREATE MODEL with text embeddings and run vector_search functions inside SQL queries triggered by each storefront request
Use Vertex AI Vector Search alone with a custom retrieval service the team writes to score and rank results for each customer query
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because building OpenSearch with custom embeddings on GKE reintroduces exactly the kind of self-managed infrastructure Cymbal's modernization aims to eliminate, and it lacks retail-specific ranking signals. Option B is correct because Vertex AI Search for commerce (also marketed as Discovery AI for retail) is Google's purpose-built product-discovery service: it ingests retail product catalogs, supports natural-language queries, and provides retail-tuned ranking, recommendations, and merchandising controls out of the box, exactly matching the case requirement. Option C is wrong because BigQuery is an analytical warehouse; running vector search inside SQL on every storefront query would create latency and cost characteristics that are inappropriate for an interactive shopping experience. Option D is wrong because Vector Search alone is a vector database; it does not include retail-specific ranking, browse, recommendations, or merchandising controls, so the team would still have to build the discovery layer themselves.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because building OpenSearch with custom embeddings on GKE reintroduces exactly the kind of self-managed infrastructure Cymbal's modernization aims to eliminate, and it lacks retail-specific ranking signals.

Option B is correct because Vertex AI Search for commerce (also marketed as Discovery AI for retail) is Google's purpose-built product-discovery service: it ingests retail product catalogs, supports natural-language queries, and provides retail-tuned ranking, recommendations, and merchandising controls out of the box, exactly matching the case requirement.

Option C is wrong because BigQuery is an analytical warehouse; running vector search inside SQL on every storefront query would create latency and cost characteristics that are inappropriate for an interactive shopping experience.

Option D is wrong because Vector Search alone is a vector database; it does not include retail-specific ranking, browse, recommendations, or merchandising controls, so the team would still have to build the discovery layer themselves.
questions.category: Managing and provisioning a cloud solution infrastructure

questions.question: Cymbal Retail processes customer credit-card transactions, so the new Google Cloud environment must be designed for PCI DSS compliance. The team is deciding where to place the cardholder data environment (CDE) and how to limit its scope. Which design best reduces PCI scope while still meeting business needs?
options: Run the entire storefront, catalog, and payment-processing workloads in a single project with broad IAM roles so that on-call engineers can debug across services quickly during peak shopping events
Place all GKE clusters and Cloud SQL instances into one shared VPC and one project, relying on Cloud Audit Logs alone to demonstrate PCI segmentation to assessors
Isolate payment processing into a dedicated project and VPC with Assured Workloads guardrails, restrict CDE access via IAM and VPC Service Controls, and tokenize PANs through a PCI-certified provider
Store full primary account numbers in Cloud SQL with column-level encryption keys managed by Cloud KMS and grant the storefront service account direct read access for performance reasons
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because broad IAM and a single shared project make every workload part of the cardholder data environment, expanding PCI scope to the entire estate. Option B is wrong because a single VPC and project with only audit logs does not provide the network segmentation, identity controls, or data-flow boundaries PCI DSS requires to limit CDE scope. Option C is correct because PCI scope reduction is achieved by isolating the CDE in its own project and VPC, applying strong identity boundaries with IAM and VPC Service Controls, and using tokenization so non-CDE systems hold only tokens rather than primary account numbers, which is the Google-recommended pattern for PCI-bound retail workloads. Option D is wrong because storing primary account numbers in the application database keeps the storefront inside CDE scope and increases blast radius if credentials are leaked; tokenization with a PCI-certified provider is the standard mitigation.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because broad IAM and a single shared project make every workload part of the cardholder data environment, expanding PCI scope to the entire estate.
Option B is wrong because a single VPC and project with only audit logs does not provide the network segmentation, identity controls, or data-flow boundaries PCI DSS requires to limit CDE scope.

Option C is correct because PCI scope reduction is achieved by isolating the CDE in its own project and VPC, applying strong identity boundaries with IAM and VPC Service Controls, and using tokenization so non-CDE systems hold only tokens rather than primary account numbers, which is the Google-recommended pattern for PCI-bound retail workloads.

Option D is wrong because storing primary account numbers in the application database keeps the storefront inside CDE scope and increases blast radius if credentials are leaked; tokenization with a PCI-certified provider is the standard mitigation.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail wants to make sure the supplier feeds and customer-conversation transcripts going into the generative-AI catalog and conversational-commerce pipelines do not accidentally leak personally identifiable information such as customer names, email addresses, or phone numbers into prompts and stored artifacts. Which Google Cloud capability should they apply?
options: Sensitive Data Protection (formerly Cloud DLP) inspection and de-identification templates run on supplier feeds and transcripts before they reach Vertex AI
Cloud KMS customer-managed encryption keys on the Cloud Storage buckets that hold the feeds so the data is encrypted at rest with Cymbal-managed keys throughout the pipeline
VPC Service Controls perimeter around the Vertex AI APIs so prompts and outputs cannot move outside the configured perimeter under any service account
Binary Authorization policies on the catalog-enrichment GKE workloads so only signed container images are allowed to call the Vertex AI API endpoints
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Sensitive Data Protection (formerly Cloud DLP) is Google Cloud's managed service for discovering and de-identifying sensitive data such as names, emails, phone numbers, and government IDs; running inspection and de-identification templates on supplier feeds and conversation transcripts before they reach Vertex AI prevents PII from being included in prompts and artifacts. Option B is wrong because customer-managed encryption keys protect data at rest but do nothing to keep PII out of prompt text or generated outputs. Option C is wrong because VPC Service Controls restrict where data can be sent but do not classify or remove PII within data that is allowed to flow. Option D is wrong because Binary Authorization controls which container images can run, not what payload they send; it does not address PII inside prompts.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because Sensitive Data Protection (formerly Cloud DLP) is Google Cloud's managed service for discovering and de-identifying sensitive data such as names, emails, phone numbers, and government IDs; running inspection and de-identification templates on supplier feeds and conversation transcripts before they reach Vertex AI prevents PII from being included in prompts and artifacts.
Option B is wrong because customer-managed encryption keys protect data at rest but do nothing to keep PII out of prompt text or generated outputs.
Option C is wrong because VPC Service Controls restrict where data can be sent but do not classify or remove PII within data that is allowed to flow.
Option D is wrong because Binary Authorization controls which container images can run, not what payload they send; it does not address PII inside prompts.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail's HITL associates need a web UI where they can review and approve gen-AI-generated product attributes, descriptions, and images before the catalog is updated. The UI must be accessible only to authenticated Cymbal employees, and the team wants to avoid exposing it on the public internet without an identity check. Which approach best fits?
options: Deploy the HITL UI to Cloud Run and front it with Identity-Aware Proxy, granting access through the company's Cloud Identity group for retail associates
Deploy the HITL UI to App Engine and rely on a long, hard-to-guess URL so only associates who have been told the URL can reach the review screens
Deploy the HITL UI as a public Cloud Run service and put a self-managed username-and-password form on the landing page for associates
Deploy the HITL UI to an internal-only GKE Service and require associates to use a VPN client whenever they need to review generated catalog content
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Identity-Aware Proxy enforces authentication and authorization for HTTPS applications at the edge using Cloud Identity, supports group-based access for the HITL associate role, and works natively with Cloud Run, App Engine, GKE, and Compute Engine backends, removing the need to build authentication into the application. Option B is wrong because security by URL obscurity provides no real protection; anyone who learns or guesses the URL reaches the UI. Option C is wrong because rolling a custom username and password form recreates well-understood authentication risks (password storage, MFA, account lockout) and bypasses Cymbal's existing identity platform. Option D is wrong because VPN-only access is operationally heavier than IAP, ties UI access to a network appliance, and still does not provide per-user authorization controls without additional identity integration.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because Identity-Aware Proxy enforces authentication and authorization for HTTPS applications at the edge using Cloud Identity, supports group-based access for the HITL associate role, and works natively with Cloud Run, App Engine, GKE, and Compute Engine backends, removing the need to build authentication into the application.
Option B is wrong because security by URL obscurity provides no real protection; anyone who learns or guesses the URL reaches the UI.

Option C is wrong because rolling a custom username and password form recreates well-understood authentication risks (password storage, MFA, account lockout) and bypasses Cymbal's existing identity platform.

Option D is wrong because VPN-only access is operationally heavier than IAP, ties UI access to a network appliance, and still does not provide per-user authorization controls without additional identity integration.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail's leadership has approved retiring the legacy file-based ETL and SFTP integrations in favor of a modern CI/CD-driven data pipeline. The architecture team must propose changes that reduce manual work and align with the modernization goals. Which TWO actions together best support this transition? (Choose two.)
options: Lift-and-shift the existing SFTP server and ETL scripts into Compute Engine VMs and leave the cron schedule and processing logic unchanged for the foreseeable future
Keep the existing ETL on-premises and add a one-way Cloud Interconnect-based replica into BigQuery so cloud-side reports and dashboards can read the same data
Re-implement supplier ingestion as event-driven pipelines using Cloud Storage triggers, Eventarc, and Cloud Run services managed in Git and deployed via Cloud Build
Adopt Cloud Build to build and test catalog-enrichment containers, with Cloud Deploy promoting them across dev, staging, and production GKE environments
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because moving SFTP and the legacy ETL logic to VMs preserves the manual error-prone process Cymbal is trying to retire; it is lift-and-shift without modernization. Option B is wrong because keeping the legacy ETL on-premises and just replicating its output postpones the modernization rather than achieving it, and it does not reduce the underlying manual effort. Option C is correct because event-driven ingestion through Cloud Storage triggers, Eventarc, and Cloud Run replaces scheduled SFTP and batch ETL with declarative, source-controlled services and removes manual file handling. Option D is correct because Cloud Build with Cloud Deploy gives Cymbal a managed CI/CD platform with promotion across environments and replaces ad-hoc deployments to GKE, which is exactly what the technical-stack modernization goal calls for.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because moving SFTP and the legacy ETL logic to VMs preserves the manual error-prone process Cymbal is trying to retire; it is lift-and-shift without modernization.
Option B is wrong because keeping the legacy ETL on-premises and just replicating its output postpones the modernization rather than achieving it, and it does not reduce the underlying manual effort.

Option C is correct because event-driven ingestion through Cloud Storage triggers, Eventarc, and Cloud Run replaces scheduled SFTP and batch ETL with declarative, source-controlled services and removes manual file handling.

Option D is correct because Cloud Build with Cloud Deploy gives Cymbal a managed CI/CD platform with promotion across environments and replaces ad-hoc deployments to GKE, which is exactly what the technical-stack modernization goal calls for.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Cymbal Retail is preparing a business case to justify retiring on-premises database hosting in favor of managed Google Cloud services. The CFO wants the analysis to include both direct cost savings and the operational impact on staff. Which framing best represents the total economic case the architect should present?
options: Compare only the monthly compute price between on-prem hardware depreciation and the equivalent Cloud SQL instance type, ignoring storage, networking, and labor costs across the entire three-year horizon
Compare only the rack and power costs of the existing data center against Cloud SQL list price, since labor will not change after migration in this team's experience

Compare full TCO (hardware refresh, facilities, licensing, backup and DR labor, headcount) against managed-service pricing with sustained-use and committed-use discounts, and quantify staff time freed
Compare the headline list price of Cloud SQL against a vendor-quoted price for an on-prem appliance refresh, assuming both run at 100% utilization for three years across all environments
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because comparing only monthly compute price ignores the dominant costs of running databases on-prem (storage, networking, backup, and most importantly labor) and the cloud discounts available for steady-state workloads. Option B is wrong because labor does change after migration: managed services remove patching, HA configuration, and backup tooling work, and any analysis that asserts labor stays constant is wrong. Option C is correct because a proper TCO comparison covers hardware refresh, facilities, licensing, backup and DR labor, and headcount versus managed-service pricing including sustained-use and committed-use discounts, and it quantifies the operational hours freed for higher-value work, which matches the case's cost-reduction business requirement. Option D is wrong because comparing list price to an idealized on-prem quote at 100% utilization for three years overstates on-prem efficiency and ignores discount programs and elasticity that change the comparison materially.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because comparing only monthly compute price ignores the dominant costs of running databases on-prem (storage, networking, backup, and most importantly labor) and the cloud discounts available for steady-state workloads.
Option B is wrong because labor does change after migration: managed services remove patching, HA configuration, and backup tooling work, and any analysis that asserts labor stays constant is wrong.

Option C is correct because a proper TCO comparison covers hardware refresh, facilities, licensing, backup and DR labor, and headcount versus managed-service pricing including sustained-use and committed-use discounts, and it quantifies the operational hours freed for higher-value work, which matches the case's cost-reduction business requirement.

Option D is wrong because comparing list price to an idealized on-prem quote at 100% utilization for three years overstates on-prem efficiency and ignores discount programs and elasticity that change the comparison materially.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Cymbal Retail wants to expose the modernized product catalog through a managed API to internal mobile, web, and partner channels. Requirements include consistent authentication, per-consumer quota and rate limiting, API versioning, and developer-portal documentation. Which Google Cloud service should they adopt?
options: Apigee, publishing the catalog API with API key and OAuth auth, per-consumer quotas, versioning, and a developer portal
An external Application Load Balancer with Cloud Armor rules manually maintained per consumer to enforce quotas and per-route rate limits across the catalog endpoints
Cloud Endpoints with ESPv2 sidecars on each catalog service plus bespoke developer documentation hosted on a separate Cloud Run static site for partners
Direct exposure of the catalog GKE Service through a public Ingress with quota and rate-limit enforcement implemented separately inside each catalog microservice
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Apigee is Google Cloud's full API management platform: it provides authentication policies (API key, OAuth), per-consumer quota and rate limiting, versioning, traffic management, and a developer portal out of the box, which is exactly the surface area Cymbal needs to expose the catalog API to internal and partner channels. Option B is wrong because an Application Load Balancer with Cloud Armor handles traffic and security policies but is not an API management product; it does not deliver consumer-level quotas, developer portals, or versioning workflows. Option C is wrong because Cloud Endpoints with ESPv2 is a lighter-weight API gateway and does not include the developer-portal, monetization, and lifecycle features Apigee provides; building those features on the side recreates Apigee badly. Option D is wrong because pushing rate-limit and quota logic into each microservice makes them inconsistent and harder to govern, and it provides no developer-facing surface.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because Apigee is Google Cloud's full API management platform: it provides authentication policies (API key, OAuth), per-consumer quota and rate limiting, versioning, traffic management, and a developer portal out of the box, which is exactly the surface area Cymbal needs to expose the catalog API to internal and partner channels.

Option B is wrong because an Application Load Balancer with Cloud Armor handles traffic and security policies but is not an API management product; it does not deliver consumer-level quotas, developer portals, or versioning workflows.

Option C is wrong because Cloud Endpoints with ESPv2 is a lighter-weight API gateway and does not include the developer-portal, monetization, and lifecycle features Apigee provides; building those features on the side recreates Apigee badly.
Option D is wrong because pushing rate-limit and quota logic into each microservice makes them inconsistent and harder to govern, and it provides no developer-facing surface.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail must migrate its on-premises MySQL, Microsoft SQL Server, and MongoDB databases to managed services on Google Cloud with minimum downtime. The team wants a single Google-managed migration tool that supports the relational sources and uses continuous change-data-capture to keep source and target in sync until cutover. Which approach should they take?
options: Use Datastream to stream change events into BigQuery and treat BigQuery as the new operational target for both the catalog and customer-order workloads going forward
Use Storage Transfer Service to copy nightly database dump files into Cloud Storage and import them into Cloud SQL during a long maintenance window for each engine
Use Database Migration Service with CDC for MySQL and SQL Server into Cloud SQL, and use MongoDB-native tools or a partner service for MongoDB
Use BigQuery Data Transfer Service to migrate all three database engines because it is presented as the single Google-managed transfer service for relational and document data
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Datastream replicates change events for analytics and integration use cases; BigQuery is not an operational store for Cymbal's transactional catalog and customer workloads. Option B is wrong because nightly dump-and-restore is the high-downtime cutover Cymbal is explicitly trying to avoid, and Storage Transfer Service is intended for object data rather than relational migration. Option C is correct because Database Migration Service is Google's managed migration service for relational engines including MySQL and Microsoft SQL Server with continuous CDC into Cloud SQL, while MongoDB requires MongoDB-native tools (or a partner) into Firestore or a managed MongoDB target, which is an honest reflection of the current product landscape. Option D is wrong because BigQuery Data Transfer Service is for loading SaaS and warehouse data into BigQuery, not for migrating operational MySQL, SQL Server, or MongoDB databases to managed cloud equivalents.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because Datastream replicates change events for analytics and integration use cases; BigQuery is not an operational store for Cymbal's transactional catalog and customer workloads.

Option B is wrong because nightly dump-and-restore is the high-downtime cutover Cymbal is explicitly trying to avoid, and Storage Transfer Service is intended for object data rather than relational migration.

Option C is correct because Database Migration Service is Google's managed migration service for relational engines including MySQL and Microsoft SQL Server with continuous CDC into Cloud SQL, while MongoDB requires MongoDB-native tools (or a partner) into Firestore or a managed MongoDB target, which is an honest reflection of the current product landscape.

Option D is wrong because BigQuery Data Transfer Service is for loading SaaS and warehouse data into BigQuery, not for migrating operational MySQL, SQL Server, or MongoDB databases to managed cloud equivalents.

questions.question: Cymbal Retail is replacing Grafana, Nagios, and Elastic with Google Cloud's native observability stack. The platform team wants to publish a service-level objective (SLO) for the new catalog-update pipeline: 99% of supplier-feed updates must propagate to the storefront within 15 minutes. Which combination of Cloud Monitoring and Cloud Logging features best implements this SLO and gives an early signal when the team is at risk of burning the error budget?
options: Build a Grafana dashboard backed by Cloud Monitoring as a data source and have an on-call associate visually check the latency graph each morning to decide whether propagation looks healthy for the past day

Configure a single Cloud Monitoring alert that fires when any catalog-update event exceeds 15 minutes in the past 5 minutes, with no SLO, no error budget, and no burn-rate policy attached to the alert
Set a Cloud Monitoring uptime check on the catalog API endpoint every 60 seconds and treat its success ratio over a 28-day window as the propagation SLO for the catalog-update pipeline
Define a Cloud Monitoring SLO with a custom SLI from Cloud Logging log-based metrics over a 28-day window, with multi-window multi-burn-rate alerts on the error budget
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because a human visually scanning a Grafana dashboard each morning is not an SLO and provides no error budget, no automated burn-rate signal, and no audit trail of compliance. Option B is wrong because a single static threshold alert is not an SLO; it lacks an error budget, does not differentiate slow burn from fast burn, and will either page constantly or miss sustained degradation. Option C is wrong because an uptime check measures probe-level reachability from monitoring infrastructure, not the end-to-end propagation latency of supplier updates the team committed to in the SLO. Option D is correct because Cloud Monitoring SLOs with custom services and SLIs built on Cloud Logging log-based metrics let the team measure the actual propagation latency they promised, set the 99% target over the recommended 28-day rolling window, and apply multi-window multi-burn-rate alerting to catch both fast and slow burn before the error budget is exhausted, which is Google's recommended SRE pattern.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because a human visually scanning a Grafana dashboard each morning is not an SLO and provides no error budget, no automated burn-rate signal, and no audit trail of compliance.

Option B is wrong because a single static threshold alert is not an SLO; it lacks an error budget, does not differentiate slow burn from fast burn, and will either page constantly or miss sustained degradation.

Option C is wrong because an uptime check measures probe-level reachability from monitoring infrastructure, not the end-to-end propagation latency of supplier updates the team committed to in the SLO.

Option D is correct because Cloud Monitoring SLOs with custom services and SLIs built on Cloud Logging log-based metrics let the team measure the actual propagation latency they promised, set the 99% target over the recommended 28-day rolling window, and apply multi-window multi-burn-rate alerting to catch both fast and slow burn before the error budget is exhausted, which is Google's recommended SRE pattern.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare must maintain its legacy file- and API-based integrations with insurance providers on-premises while moving customer-facing workloads to Google Cloud. The lease on one colocation facility is expiring soon and the technical team requires a secure, high-performance connection between the remaining on-premises systems and Google Cloud. Which connectivity option best satisfies EHR Healthcare's hybrid requirement for its production PHI traffic?
options: Provision Dedicated Interconnect between the remaining colocation facility and a Google Cloud region close to the colo, deploying redundant 10 Gbps connections across two metro availability domains for a 99.9% SLA

Use HA VPN over the public internet with two tunnels per region, accepting public-internet routing for legacy insurance interfaces because IPsec encryption is sufficient for the PHI workloads in transit
Set up Carrier Peering through a supported ISP so that EHR Healthcare can reach Google APIs from the colocation facility without provisioning private connectivity for VPC traffic to GKE
Use Direct Peering at a Google Cloud edge point of presence to exchange routes between the colocation facility and EHR Healthcare's VPC, avoiding any partner or carrier-managed circuit
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Dedicated Interconnect provides a private, high-bandwidth, low-latency physical connection between the colocation facility and Google Cloud; deploying redundant connections across two metro availability domains is the documented topology for a 99.9% SLA, which aligns with EHR Healthcare's 99.9% customer-facing availability target and the technical requirement for a secure, high-performance link. Option B is wrong because HA VPN over the public internet introduces variable latency and is not 'high-performance' for sustained PHI workloads between EHR Healthcare's colo and Google Cloud; it is an acceptable backup, not the primary link. Option C is wrong because Carrier Peering only provides access to Google public APIs (e.g., Workspace) and cannot carry traffic to VPC resources such as GKE clusters hosting customer-facing apps. Option D is wrong because Direct Peering exchanges routes only for Google public services and is not a supported path for VPC-bound private traffic; it also does not provide a Google-backed SLA for EHR Healthcare's hybrid workloads.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because Dedicated Interconnect provides a private, high-bandwidth, low-latency physical connection between the colocation facility and Google Cloud; deploying redundant connections across two metro availability domains is the documented topology for a 99.9% SLA, which aligns with EHR Healthcare's 99.9% customer-facing availability target and the technical requirement for a secure, high-performance link.

Option B is wrong because HA VPN over the public internet introduces variable latency and is not 'high-performance' for sustained PHI workloads between EHR Healthcare's colo and Google Cloud; it is an acceptable backup, not the primary link.
Option C is wrong because Carrier Peering only provides access to Google public APIs (e.g., Workspace) and cannot carry traffic to VPC resources such as GKE clusters hosting customer-facing apps.

Option D is wrong because Direct Peering exchanges routes only for Google public services and is not a supported path for VPC-bound private traffic; it also does not provide a Google-backed SLA for EHR Healthcare's hybrid workloads.
questions.category: Managing and provisioning a cloud solution infrastructure

questions.question: EHR Healthcare has recently containerized many of its customer-facing web applications onto a group of Kubernetes clusters and needs a consistent way to manage these clusters across multiple environments, including the remaining colocation facility during migration. The platform team wants centralized policy, configuration, and observability for every cluster regardless of where it runs. Which Google Cloud approach best meets this requirement?
options: Migrate every cluster to GKE Autopilot in a single region and shut down the on-premises Kubernetes clusters immediately so that EHR Healthcare only has to manage one control plane during the colocation lease wind-down

Standardize on self-managed Kubernetes installed on Compute Engine MIGs in each environment, scripting upgrades and policy with Ansible so EHR Healthcare retains full control of every component of the control plane

Register the existing on-premises clusters and the new GKE clusters into a GKE Enterprise (Anthos) fleet, then apply Config Sync and Policy Controller from a central config repository to enforce consistent configuration and policies across all environments

Deploy a separate Cloud Run service for each containerized application and decommission the Kubernetes clusters, accepting that the on-premises insurance integrations will keep running in the colocation facility unchanged
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because EHR Healthcare cannot immediately shut down on-premises clusters: the legacy insurance interfaces remain on-premises and customer-facing workloads must keep running during migration, so a single-region GKE Autopilot target ignores the multi-environment requirement. Option B is wrong because self-managed Kubernetes on Compute Engine reintroduces the heavy operational burden EHR Healthcare is trying to leave behind in the colocation facility and provides no centralized policy or configuration plane across environments. Option C is correct because GKE Enterprise (Anthos) fleets are the documented Google-recommended way to manage multiple Kubernetes clusters across Google Cloud and on-premises with a consistent control plane, Config Sync for GitOps configuration, and Policy Controller for guardrails, directly satisfying EHR Healthcare's requirements for consistent management and multiple container-based environments. Option D is wrong because rewriting every containerized workload to Cloud Run abandons the recent containerization investment, does not address multi-environment Kubernetes management, and removes Kubernetes-level controls EHR Healthcare's platform team already operates.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because EHR Healthcare cannot immediately shut down on-premises clusters: the legacy insurance interfaces remain on-premises and customer-facing workloads must keep running during migration, so a single-region GKE Autopilot target ignores the multi-environment requirement.

Option B is wrong because self-managed Kubernetes on Compute Engine reintroduces the heavy operational burden EHR Healthcare is trying to leave behind in the colocation facility and provides no centralized policy or configuration plane across environments.

Option C is correct because GKE Enterprise (Anthos) fleets are the documented Google-recommended way to manage multiple Kubernetes clusters across Google Cloud and on-premises with a consistent control plane, Config Sync for GitOps configuration, and Policy Controller for guardrails, directly satisfying EHR Healthcare's requirements for consistent management and multiple container-based environments.

Option D is wrong because rewriting every containerized workload to Cloud Run abandons the recent containerization investment, does not address multi-environment Kubernetes management, and removes Kubernetes-level controls EHR Healthcare's platform team already operates.
questions.category: Designing and planning a cloud solution architecture

questions.question: EHR Healthcare's existing data layer includes a large MySQL footprint used by its containerized customer-facing applications. The architecture team wants the lowest operational burden, automated backups, point-in-time recovery, and built-in high availability that supports the 99.9% customer-facing SLA without rewriting the schema. Which target should EHR Healthcare select for its MySQL workloads on Google Cloud?
options: Run MySQL on Compute Engine instances behind a regional managed instance group, scripting binlog backups to Cloud Storage and configuring Pacemaker for failover to retain the same operational pattern used in the colocation facility

Migrate the MySQL databases to Spanner using a custom application-side ORM rewrite so EHR Healthcare can benefit from external strong consistency across continents, even though the schema must be redesigned
Move the MySQL data into BigQuery so it can also be queried for healthcare trend insights, and let customer-facing applications issue transactional queries through the BigQuery Storage Write API

Migrate the MySQL databases to Cloud SQL for MySQL with the Enterprise Plus edition and a regional (high-availability) configuration, enabling automated backups and point-in-time recovery to meet the 99.9% SLA
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because self-managed MySQL on Compute Engine reproduces the high-operational-cost pattern EHR Healthcare wants to leave behind in the colocation facility and conflicts with the business requirement to decrease infrastructure administration costs. Option B is wrong because Spanner is a different data model that requires schema and application changes; EHR Healthcare's stated goal is to migrate MySQL without rewriting applications, and Spanner's strengths are not required for typical EHR workloads. Option C is wrong because BigQuery is an analytical warehouse, not a transactional OLTP store; using it for customer-facing transactional MySQL traffic is an architectural mismatch even though BigQuery is useful for the separate healthcare-trends analytics requirement. Option D is correct because Cloud SQL for MySQL (Enterprise Plus) with a regional HA configuration is the Google-recommended managed-MySQL target: automated backups, point-in-time recovery, automatic failover within the region, and minimal operational overhead, all of which support EHR Healthcare's 99.9% customer-facing SLA and lower-infrastructure-administration-cost requirement.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because self-managed MySQL on Compute Engine reproduces the high-operational-cost pattern EHR Healthcare wants to leave behind in the colocation facility and conflicts with the business requirement to decrease infrastructure administration costs.

Option B is wrong because Spanner is a different data model that requires schema and application changes; EHR Healthcare's stated goal is to migrate MySQL without rewriting applications, and Spanner's strengths are not required for typical EHR workloads.

Option C is wrong because BigQuery is an analytical warehouse, not a transactional OLTP store; using it for customer-facing transactional MySQL traffic is an architectural mismatch even though BigQuery is useful for the separate healthcare-trends analytics requirement.

Option D is correct because Cloud SQL for MySQL (Enterprise Plus) with a regional HA configuration is the Google-recommended managed-MySQL target: automated backups, point-in-time recovery, automatic failover within the region, and minimal operational overhead, all of which support EHR Healthcare's 99.9% customer-facing SLA and lower-infrastructure-administration-cost requirement.
questions.category: Designing and planning a cloud solution architecture

questions.question: EHR Healthcare must provide a minimum of 99.9% availability for all customer-facing systems, and the new GKE-based platform on Google Cloud must keep operating during a regional impairment. The architecture team is finalizing the disaster recovery design for the customer-facing tier and its supporting data layer. Which two design decisions are most appropriate? (Choose two.)
options: Run customer-facing GKE clusters as zonal clusters in a single zone with daily snapshots restored to a different region on demand to keep costs low while still being able to recover within the 99.9% allowance
Configure Cloud SQL for MySQL with cross-region read replicas and document a tested replica-promotion runbook so the OLTP tier can be recovered in a second region during a regional outage

Keep MongoDB and Redis writable only from on-premises during the migration window and treat the cloud copies as cold backups, restoring them manually from snapshots only if both colocation facilities fail

Deploy each customer-facing GKE workload as a regional GKE cluster in two regions and front them with a global external Application Load Balancer that automatically fails over between regional backends
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because zonal GKE clusters offer only zonal availability and on-demand snapshot restore to another region produces RTOs that easily violate 99.9%; this fails the SLA. Option B is correct because Cloud SQL for MySQL cross-region read replicas (with a tested promotion runbook) give EHR Healthcare a documented path to recover the OLTP tier in a second region during a regional outage, complementing the regional HA configuration. Option C is wrong because leaving MongoDB and Redis writable only on-premises during migration removes the cloud DR path, contradicts the goal of replacing the colocation facilities, and exposes EHR Healthcare to a single-site failure for those data stores. Option D is correct because EHR Healthcare's 99.9% customer-facing SLA and the technical requirement to dynamically scale and provision new environments are best satisfied by regional GKE clusters in at least two regions fronted by a global external Application Load Balancer, which provides automatic regional failover for the containerized customer-facing apps.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because zonal GKE clusters offer only zonal availability and on-demand snapshot restore to another region produces RTOs that easily violate 99.9%; this fails the SLA.

Option B is correct because Cloud SQL for MySQL cross-region read replicas (with a tested promotion runbook) give EHR Healthcare a documented path to recover the OLTP tier in a second region during a regional outage, complementing the regional HA configuration.

Option C is wrong because leaving MongoDB and Redis writable only on-premises during migration removes the cloud DR path, contradicts the goal of replacing the colocation facilities, and exposes EHR Healthcare to a single-site failure for those data stores.

Option D is correct because EHR Healthcare's 99.9% customer-facing SLA and the technical requirement to dynamically scale and provision new environments are best satisfied by regional GKE clusters in at least two regions fronted by a global external Application Load Balancer, which provides automatic regional failover for the containerized customer-facing apps.
questions.category: Designing and planning a cloud solution architecture

questions.question: EHR Healthcare's data stores include a Microsoft SQL Server footprint that backs several customer-facing applications. The team wants to migrate SQL Server to a managed service on Google Cloud with minimal application changes, native Windows authentication support, and automated patching. Which target should EHR Healthcare choose for the MS SQL Server workloads?
options: Self-host SQL Server Always On availability groups on Compute Engine Windows VMs in two regions, scripting backups to Cloud Storage so the database team can keep using the same on-premises operations playbook
Refactor every SQL Server-bound application to PostgreSQL and move the data to AlloyDB for PostgreSQL so EHR Healthcare can standardize on a single open-source engine across the platform
Migrate the MS SQL Server databases to Cloud SQL for SQL Server with a regional HA configuration, taking advantage of automated backups, point-in-time recovery, and managed patching
Move the SQL Server schema and data into BigQuery so analytics and OLTP queries can share the same warehouse, eliminating the need for a dedicated relational database tier
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because self-hosting SQL Server Always On availability groups on Compute Engine retains heavy operational overhead and contradicts EHR Healthcare's goal to decrease infrastructure administration costs. Option B is wrong because rewriting every SQL Server application to PostgreSQL is a major refactor that EHR Healthcare has no stated capacity for and removes Windows-auth compatibility for customer-facing apps. Option C is correct because Cloud SQL for SQL Server with a regional HA configuration is the documented managed target for MS SQL Server: it provides automated backups, point-in-time recovery, managed patching, and Windows authentication support, matching EHR Healthcare's requirement to lower operational burden without rewriting applications. Option D is wrong because BigQuery is an analytical warehouse, not a transactional OLTP engine, and using it to back customer-facing SQL Server applications is an architectural mismatch even though BigQuery may serve EHR Healthcare's separate analytics needs.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because self-hosting SQL Server Always On availability groups on Compute Engine retains heavy operational overhead and contradicts EHR Healthcare's goal to decrease infrastructure administration costs.

Option B is wrong because rewriting every SQL Server application to PostgreSQL is a major refactor that EHR Healthcare has no stated capacity for and removes Windows-auth compatibility for customer-facing apps.

Option C is correct because Cloud SQL for SQL Server with a regional HA configuration is the documented managed target for MS SQL Server: it provides automated backups, point-in-time recovery, managed patching, and Windows authentication support, matching EHR Healthcare's requirement to lower operational burden without rewriting applications.

Option D is wrong because BigQuery is an analytical warehouse, not a transactional OLTP engine, and using it to back customer-facing SQL Server applications is an architectural mismatch even though BigQuery may serve EHR Healthcare's separate analytics needs.
questions.category: Designing and planning a cloud solution architecture

questions.question: EHR Healthcare must maintain regulatory compliance for protected health information (PHI) stored and processed in its electronic health record SaaS. The compliance team is preparing the move to Google Cloud and asks the architect for the most accurate description of what is required so that EHR Healthcare can operate HIPAA-regulated workloads on Google Cloud. Which statement best reflects what EHR Healthcare must do?
options: Because Google Cloud is HIPAA-certified end to end, EHR Healthcare can store PHI in any Google Cloud product without further action as long as the data is encrypted at rest using Google-managed keys
EHR Healthcare must restrict PHI to Google Workspace and BigQuery only, because those are the only Google Cloud products that support a Business Associate Addendum (BAA) for healthcare customers
EHR Healthcare can rely on Cloud HSM as its sole HIPAA compliance control because HSM-backed encryption keys are equivalent to a Business Associate Addendum under HIPAA Security Rule guidance

EHR Healthcare must execute a Business Associate Addendum (BAA) with Google, restrict PHI workloads to Google Cloud products covered by the BAA, and implement its own administrative, physical, and technical safeguards on top
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because no cloud provider is 'HIPAA-certified' end to end; HIPAA compliance is a shared responsibility and requires the customer to execute a BAA and configure services correctly. Option B is wrong because the BAA-covered list is much broader than just Workspace and BigQuery, and includes the core compute, storage, networking, database, and security products EHR Healthcare relies on (e.g., GKE, Cloud SQL, Cloud Storage, Cloud Logging). Option C is wrong because Cloud HSM is a single technical control; it does not substitute for the BAA and does not by itself constitute HIPAA compliance. Option D is correct because HIPAA on Google Cloud requires that EHR Healthcare execute a Business Associate Addendum with Google, keep PHI workloads on the BAA-covered services, and implement its own administrative, physical, and technical safeguards (access controls, audit logging, encryption configuration, training, incident response) — this matches Google's documented shared-responsibility model and EHR Healthcare's regulatory-compliance business requirement.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because no cloud provider is 'HIPAA-certified' end to end; HIPAA compliance is a shared responsibility and requires the customer to execute a BAA and configure services correctly.

Option B is wrong because the BAA-covered list is much broader than just Workspace and BigQuery, and includes the core compute, storage, networking, database, and security products EHR Healthcare relies on (e.g., GKE, Cloud SQL, Cloud Storage, Cloud Logging).
Option C is wrong because Cloud HSM is a single technical control; it does not substitute for the BAA and does not by itself constitute HIPAA compliance.

Option D is correct because HIPAA on Google Cloud requires that EHR Healthcare execute a Business Associate Addendum with Google, keep PHI workloads on the BAA-covered services, and implement its own administrative, physical, and technical safeguards (access controls, audit logging, encryption configuration, training, incident response) — this matches Google's documented shared-responsibility model and EHR Healthcare's regulatory-compliance business requirement.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare wants to prevent PHI stored in Cloud Storage and BigQuery from being exfiltrated from its production project, even if a user with valid IAM credentials is phished. The security team needs a perimeter control that blocks data movement outside an approved set of projects regardless of identity. Which Google Cloud capability should the team implement?
options: Configure VPC Service Controls around the production project to define a service perimeter that includes Cloud Storage and BigQuery and blocks data movement to projects outside the perimeter
Rely on uniform bucket-level access on Cloud Storage and column-level IAM on BigQuery, because consistent IAM policy is sufficient to prevent data exfiltration without a perimeter control
Enable Access Transparency logs and rotate Google-managed encryption keys monthly so EHR Healthcare can detect exfiltration after it happens, satisfying the regulatory-compliance requirement
Replace all PHI storage with Cloud HSM-managed encrypted blobs and let only client-side decryption happen on customer laptops, removing the need for any network or perimeter control
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because VPC Service Controls create an identity-independent service perimeter around Google-managed services like Cloud Storage and BigQuery, blocking data movement to projects outside the perimeter even when valid IAM credentials are presented — the exact control EHR Healthcare needs to mitigate phished-credential exfiltration of PHI. Option B is wrong because IAM alone (including uniform bucket-level access and column-level IAM) is identity-based and is bypassed when an attacker has valid credentials; it does not implement a perimeter. Option C is wrong because Access Transparency provides post-hoc visibility into Google support access, not a preventive control against customer-side exfiltration, and key rotation does not prevent exfiltration. Option D is wrong because client-side encryption with HSM-managed keys does not stop a credentialed user from exporting ciphertext-plus-decryption-context out of the perimeter, and it abandons managed Google Cloud data services that EHR Healthcare already depends on.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because VPC Service Controls create an identity-independent service perimeter around Google-managed services like Cloud Storage and BigQuery, blocking data movement to projects outside the perimeter even when valid IAM credentials are presented — the exact control EHR Healthcare needs to mitigate phished-credential exfiltration of PHI.

Option B is wrong because IAM alone (including uniform bucket-level access and column-level IAM) is identity-based and is bypassed when an attacker has valid credentials; it does not implement a perimeter.

Option C is wrong because Access Transparency provides post-hoc visibility into Google support access, not a preventive control against customer-side exfiltration, and key rotation does not prevent exfiltration.

Option D is wrong because client-side encryption with HSM-managed keys does not stop a credentialed user from exporting ciphertext-plus-decryption-context out of the perimeter, and it abandons managed Google Cloud data services that EHR Healthcare already depends on.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare's users are managed via Microsoft Active Directory on-premises. The platform team wants employees to keep using their existing AD credentials to access Google Cloud resources during and after the migration, without manually creating Cloud Identity users. Which approach is the most appropriate identity strategy?
options: Provision Cloud Identity by configuring Google Cloud Directory Sync (GCDS) to provision users and groups from on-premises Active Directory, then federate authentication to AD via SAML so employees use AD credentials to sign in
Export the Active Directory user list to a CSV file once and manually create matching Cloud Identity users for every employee, then maintain the mapping spreadsheet whenever AD changes occur
Skip Cloud Identity entirely and grant every employee access to Google Cloud through their personal Google account, mapping permissions by email convention so AD remains the only identity store
Run Active Directory Domain Services on Compute Engine and have every Google Cloud resource authenticate users directly through LDAP, eliminating the need to federate identities with Cloud Identity
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Google Cloud Directory Sync (GCDS) provisions users and groups from on-premises Active Directory into Cloud Identity, and SAML-based federation to AD (typically via AD Federation Services) lets EHR Healthcare's employees sign in to Google Cloud with their existing AD credentials. This is the documented Google pattern for AD-backed identity and meets EHR Healthcare's stated environment without rebuilding the directory. Option B is wrong because manual CSV-driven user provisioning does not scale, drifts immediately, and leaves no automated lifecycle (joiner/mover/leaver) integration with AD. Option C is wrong because relying on personal Google accounts bypasses corporate identity controls, breaks auditability, and does not satisfy EHR Healthcare's regulatory-compliance posture. Option D is wrong because Google Cloud services authenticate against Cloud Identity, not LDAP directly; running AD DS on Compute Engine still requires GCDS plus federation for cloud sign-in.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because Google Cloud Directory Sync (GCDS) provisions users and groups from on-premises Active Directory into Cloud Identity, and SAML-based federation to AD (typically via AD Federation Services) lets EHR Healthcare's employees sign in to Google Cloud with their existing AD credentials. This is the documented Google pattern for AD-backed identity and meets EHR Healthcare's stated environment without rebuilding the directory.
Option B is wrong because manual CSV-driven user provisioning does not scale, drifts immediately, and leaves no automated lifecycle (joiner/mover/leaver) integration with AD.
Option C is wrong because relying on personal Google accounts bypasses corporate identity controls, breaks auditability, and does not satisfy EHR Healthcare's regulatory-compliance posture.
Option D is wrong because Google Cloud services authenticate against Cloud Identity, not LDAP directly; running AD DS on Compute Engine still requires GCDS plus federation for cloud sign-in.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare's compliance team requires that the encryption keys protecting PHI at rest be controlled by EHR Healthcare and rotated on a fixed schedule independent of Google. The architect must select an encryption-key approach that works across Cloud Storage, BigQuery, Cloud SQL, and Persistent Disks used by the GKE workloads. Which approach is the most appropriate?
options: Configure Cloud KMS with customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) and apply them to the relevant Cloud Storage buckets, BigQuery datasets, Cloud SQL instances, and Persistent Disks, automating key rotation through a defined CMEK rotation schedule

Continue using Google-managed default encryption keys for every service and treat the existing AES-256 at-rest encryption as sufficient because EHR Healthcare's BAA already covers cryptographic controls

Use Secret Manager to store a single AES key as a secret and have each application read the secret to perform client-side encryption of PHI before writing to any storage backend, removing any need for KMS
Configure each Cloud Storage bucket with customer-supplied encryption keys (CSEK) only and accept that BigQuery, Cloud SQL, and Persistent Disks will keep using default Google-managed keys
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Cloud KMS with customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) is supported across Cloud Storage, BigQuery, Cloud SQL, and Persistent Disks, and CMEK with a configured rotation schedule gives EHR Healthcare ownership of the key material and rotation cadence required by its compliance team. Option B is wrong because while default Google-managed encryption is real, it does not give EHR Healthcare control over the key or rotation schedule and so does not satisfy the stated requirement. Option C is wrong because pushing client-side AES encryption to every application is a substantial development effort, breaks integration with managed services (e.g., BigQuery analytics, Cloud SQL native indexing) and provides no centralized key lifecycle. Option D is wrong because CSEK is supported only on a narrow set of services (notably Cloud Storage and Compute Engine disks) and does not cover BigQuery or Cloud SQL CMEK requirements, leaving most of EHR Healthcare's PHI under default keys.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because Cloud KMS with customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) is supported across Cloud Storage, BigQuery, Cloud SQL, and Persistent Disks, and CMEK with a configured rotation schedule gives EHR Healthcare ownership of the key material and rotation cadence required by its compliance team.
Option B is wrong because while default Google-managed encryption is real, it does not give EHR Healthcare control over the key or rotation schedule and so does not satisfy the stated requirement.

Option C is wrong because pushing client-side AES encryption to every application is a substantial development effort, breaks integration with managed services (e.g., BigQuery analytics, Cloud SQL native indexing) and provides no centralized key lifecycle.

Option D is wrong because CSEK is supported only on a narrow set of services (notably Cloud Storage and Compute Engine disks) and does not cover BigQuery or Cloud SQL CMEK requirements, leaving most of EHR Healthcare's PHI under default keys.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare commits to a minimum 99.9% availability for all customer-facing systems and the SRE team is publishing the first Service Level Objective (SLO) for the GKE-based patient portal. Which approach best defines this initial availability SLO in a way that reflects the business commitment and real user experience?
options: Define the SLO as the ratio of customer HTTP requests to the patient portal that return a non-5xx status within a documented latency budget over total valid requests, with a 99.9% target measured over a rolling 28-day window
Define the SLO as the percentage of Cloud Monitoring uptime checks from a single region that return a 200 over the last 24 hours, alerting whenever any probe fails so the team always responds quickly
Define the SLO as the CPU utilization of the GKE node pool staying below 80% for 99.9% of the time, since saturation is the strongest leading indicator of patient-portal availability
Defer publishing an SLO for 12 months until the team has accumulated a full year of production traffic, at which point a statistically rigorous target can be derived from long-term trends
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because a customer-facing SLO should be expressed as a ratio of good events to total events using real user traffic, with a defined latency budget and a rolling measurement window; the 99.9% target and 28-day window match EHR Healthcare's business commitment and SRE practice. Option B is wrong because synthetic uptime checks measure probe reachability, not real user traffic, and alerting on every probe failure ignores the error-budget framework. Option C is wrong because CPU utilization is a saturation signal, not a user-facing availability indicator; high CPU does not mean requests are succeeding. Option D is wrong because delaying SLOs for 12 months prevents EHR Healthcare from using error budgets to make engineering and rollout decisions; SRE practice is to publish an initial SLO based on baseline data and iterate.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because a customer-facing SLO should be expressed as a ratio of good events to total events using real user traffic, with a defined latency budget and a rolling measurement window; the 99.9% target and 28-day window match EHR Healthcare's business commitment and SRE practice.
Option B is wrong because synthetic uptime checks measure probe reachability, not real user traffic, and alerting on every probe failure ignores the error-budget framework.
Option C is wrong because CPU utilization is a saturation signal, not a user-facing availability indicator; high CPU does not mean requests are succeeding.

Option D is wrong because delaying SLOs for 12 months prevents EHR Healthcare from using error budgets to make engineering and rollout decisions; SRE practice is to publish an initial SLO based on baseline data and iterate.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare's monitoring is currently done via various open-source tools, and alerts are sent via email and are often ignored. The SRE lead is migrating to Cloud Operations and wants to make alert fatigue measurably lower by aligning alerts to error-budget consumption on the 99.9% patient-portal SLO. Which alerting configuration most faithfully implements the SRE workbook recommendation for a 'fast burn' page on a 30-day SLO window?
options: Configure a multi-window, multi-burn-rate alert that pages on-call when the 1-hour burn rate exceeds 14.4x and the 5-minute burn rate also exceeds 14.4x, corresponding to consuming 2% of the 30-day error budget inside the short window
Send an email alert any time the 24-hour request error rate rises above 0.1% so that any small breach over a long window triggers a notification to the entire engineering distribution list
Page on-call any time the 30-day rolling SLO compliance drops below 99.9%, ensuring that no alert fires until the customer-facing SLA is already breached and customers are clearly impacted
Page on-call any time a single GKE node reports above 80% CPU for one minute, because saturation correlates closely enough with portal availability to act as a leading SLO signal
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because multi-window, multi-burn-rate alerting (e.g., 1-hour burn rate gated by a 5-minute burn rate, both above 14.4x for a 30-day 99.9% SLO) is the documented SRE workbook recommendation for a fast-burn page: it catches rapid budget consumption (~2% in the alerting window) without firing on transient blips, directly attacking the alert-fatigue problem at EHR Healthcare. Option B is wrong because email-only alerts on a long 24-hour window reproduce the exact failure mode EHR Healthcare is trying to escape (ignored email alerts) and do not align to error-budget burn. Option C is wrong because alerting only when the 30-day SLO is already breached defeats the purpose of an SLO — by then customers are impacted and the error budget is exhausted. Option D is wrong because CPU saturation is not a user-facing SLI; paging on node CPU bypasses the SLO entirely and reintroduces noisy operational alerts.
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because multi-window, multi-burn-rate alerting (e.g., 1-hour burn rate gated by a 5-minute burn rate, both above 14.4x for a 30-day 99.9% SLO) is the documented SRE workbook recommendation for a fast-burn page: it catches rapid budget consumption (~2% in the alerting window) without firing on transient blips, directly attacking the alert-fatigue problem at EHR Healthcare.
Option B is wrong because email-only alerts on a long 24-hour window reproduce the exact failure mode EHR Healthcare is trying to escape (ignored email alerts) and do not align to error-budget burn.
Option C is wrong because alerting only when the 30-day SLO is already breached defeats the purpose of an SLO — by then customers are impacted and the error budget is exhausted.
Option D is wrong because CPU saturation is not a user-facing SLI; paging on node CPU bypasses the SLO entirely and reintroduces noisy operational alerts.

questions.question: EHR Healthcare's data layer is a mixture of MySQL, MS SQL Server, Redis, and MongoDB hosted in the colocation facilities. The migration architect needs to move these databases to Google Cloud and is selecting target services and migration tools. Which two statements describe the most appropriate combinations? (Choose two.)
options: Migrate MongoDB to BigQuery by exporting collections to JSON every night and treat BigQuery as the new operational store, since BigQuery now supports MongoDB-compatible queries for live workloads

Use Database Migration Service to perform a minimal-downtime migration of MySQL and SQL Server into Cloud SQL for MySQL and Cloud SQL for SQL Server, taking advantage of native change-data-capture for the cutover
Migrate every database to Spanner by rewriting application data access layers to use Spanner's SQL dialect, because Spanner is the only Google Cloud database that can meet a 99.9% availability target
Migrate Redis workloads to Memorystore for Redis (or Memorystore for Redis Cluster, depending on scale) and use a tool such as RIOT to perform a managed cutover from the on-premises Redis instances
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because BigQuery is an analytical warehouse, not an operational document store, and is not MongoDB-compatible for live transactional workloads; moving MongoDB to BigQuery would break customer-facing applications. Option B is correct because Database Migration Service supports MySQL into Cloud SQL for MySQL and SQL Server into Cloud SQL for SQL Server with continuous change-data-capture, allowing EHR Healthcare to perform a minimal-downtime cutover from the colocation facility — the exact pattern the migration plan needs for those two engines. Option C is wrong because Spanner is not the only database that can meet 99.9% on Google Cloud — Cloud SQL HA, Memorystore HA, and Bigtable all meet 99.9% — and forcing a rewrite of every application to Spanner is far more disruption than EHR Healthcare's plan allows. Option D is correct because Memorystore for Redis (and Memorystore for Redis Cluster for higher scale) is the documented managed target for Redis on Google Cloud, and tools such as RIOT or replication-based cutover allow EHR Healthcare to migrate the cache tier with minimal disruption.
optionExplanations: Option A is wrong because BigQuery is an analytical warehouse, not an operational document store, and is not MongoDB-compatible for live transactional workloads; moving MongoDB to BigQuery would break customer-facing applications.

Option B is correct because Database Migration Service supports MySQL into Cloud SQL for MySQL and SQL Server into Cloud SQL for SQL Server with continuous change-data-capture, allowing EHR Healthcare to perform a minimal-downtime cutover from the colocation facility — the exact pattern the migration plan needs for those two engines.

Option C is wrong because Spanner is not the only database that can meet 99.9% on Google Cloud — Cloud SQL HA, Memorystore HA, and Bigtable all meet 99.9% — and forcing a rewrite of every application to Spanner is far more disruption than EHR Healthcare's plan allows.

Option D is correct because Memorystore for Redis (and Memorystore for Redis Cluster for higher scale) is the documented managed target for Redis on Google Cloud, and tools such as RIOT or replication-based cutover allow EHR Healthcare to migrate the cache tier with minimal disruption.

questions.question: A business priority for EHR Healthcare is to on-board new insurance providers as quickly as possible by exposing standardized APIs for ingest and integration. The team wants centralized API lifecycle management, developer portal access for new partners, traffic policies, and analytics across every provider integration. Which Google Cloud product should they adopt?
options: Apigee API Management, fronting the new ingestion APIs with policies, quota and rate limiting, OAuth-based partner authentication, and a developer portal that lets insurance providers self-serve credentials and documentation
Cloud Endpoints alone with API keys distributed manually to each insurance provider over email, because Endpoints is sufficient for any provider-facing API regardless of governance requirements
